title: The Vanishing Line
text:
We are taught early to distinguish art from craft, and the distinction flatters us: art expresses, craft executes; the artist invents, the craftsman repeats; one labors for the spirit, the other for the marketplace. The cabinetmaker and the painter may share a workshop, but only one, we are assured, has access to genius. This hierarchy is so familiar that we forget how recent it is. For most of human history no such partition existed. The Greek word techne named pottery and tragedy alike; the medieval guild made no apology for the altarpiece that was also a contract fulfilled. Only in the eighteenth century did "the fine arts" detach themselves, cloaked in a new vocabulary of disinterest and originality, from the humble trades that had borne them.

The standard defense of the distinction rests on purpose. Craft, it is said, is functional: a chair must hold a body, a bowl must hold soup, and the maker's imagination is therefore subordinate to use. Art, by contrast, serves no end beyond itself; it asks only to be contemplated. The argument has a clean appeal, yet it dissolves under pressure. A cathedral is fiercely functional and unmistakably art. A poem may be written to flatter a patron or sell a war. To insist that the presence of a use disqualifies an object from art is to exile most of the world's masterpieces, which were nearly all commissioned, paid for, and put to work.

A subtler defense locates the difference not in the object but in the maker's intention, or in the response the object invites. Here I am more sympathetic, though not persuaded. It seems true that we treat certain objects as occasions for sustained, disinterested attention, and others as mere instruments we look through rather than at. But this is a fact about us, the beholders, not about the things. The same woven blanket hangs behind glass in one room and warms a bed in the next; nothing in the wool has changed. If the distinction tracks our attention rather than any property of the made thing, then it is real but not, in the way its defenders wish, fixed.

What survives the demolition is something humbler and, I think, more honest. The art-craft boundary is not a discovery about the nature of objects; it is a verdict a culture issues, and verdicts can be appealed. The discomfort this produces is instructive. We want the boundary to lie in the work because we want our judgments of value to be findings rather than choices. To admit that calling something "art" is partly an act of conferral, not detection, is to accept responsibility for the canon we assemble — to concede that the quilt excluded from the museum was excluded by us, and could be admitted by us.

None of this means the words are interchangeable or that all making is equal. A culture's verdicts are not arbitrary; they answer to skill, to the depth of attention a thing rewards, to the tradition it converses with. I am not claiming that craft and art are the same, only that the line between them is drawn by hands, not found in nature — and that a line drawn by hands tells us as much about the people holding the pencil as about the territory it divides. The honest critic, then, does not ask whether an object is "really" art. She asks what we do when we say so, and whether, this time, we should.

questions.stem: The author's central thesis is best captured by which statement?
options: The functional defense of the art-craft distinction succeeds where the intention-based defense fails.
The distinction between art and craft reflects a culture's evaluative choices rather than a fixed property of objects.
Because all masterpieces were commissioned, the categories of art and craft are meaningless and should be abandoned.
Art and craft were properly unified until the eighteenth century corrupted the concept with notions of disinterest.
questions.explanation: The passage builds to the claim that the boundary is "a verdict a culture issues" and is "drawn by hands, not found in nature," while still answering to skill and tradition — option B. Option A reverses the author, who rejects the functional defense outright ("it dissolves under pressure") and is only partly sympathetic to the intention-based one. Option C is too extreme: the author explicitly denies the words are interchangeable or that all making is equal. Option D misreads the historical paragraph; the author treats the eighteenth-century separation as recent and contingent, not as a fall from a "proper" state, and that history is context, not the thesis.

questions.stem: Which of the following does the author explicitly state in the passage?
options: The Greek word techne applied to pottery and tragedy alike.
Disinterested contemplation is the one feature that reliably separates art from craft.
Quilts have historically been of lower craftsmanship than museum paintings.
The medieval guild apologized for treating altarpieces as fulfilled contracts.
questions.explanation: The first paragraph states directly that "techne named pottery and tragedy alike" — option A. Option B contradicts the third paragraph, where the author finds the contemplation criterion real "but not... fixed" and a fact about beholders, not objects. Option C is never asserted; the quilt example concerns exclusion by cultural verdict, not inferior skill, and the author insists verdicts "answer to skill." Option D reverses the text, which says the guild "made no apology" for the altarpiece that was also a contract. Only A reproduces an explicit statement.

questions.stem: The example of the woven blanket that "hangs behind glass in one room and warms a bed in the next" functions primarily to:
options: prove that craft objects are aesthetically superior to objects displayed as art
show that an object's status can shift while its physical properties remain unchanged
concede that the intention-based defense of the distinction is ultimately correct
argue that museums should stop displaying functional objects
questions.explanation: The blanket appears in the paragraph on response and attention; the author notes "nothing in the wool has changed," using it to show status tracks beholders, not the object — option B. Option A imports a superiority claim the author never makes; the point is parity of the physical thing, not ranking. Option C inverts the example's role: it is offered as a reason the author remains "not persuaded" that the intention/response defense fixes the distinction. Option D is out of scope — the passage draws no policy conclusion about museum display. B alone captures the illustrative function.

questions.stem: The passage suggests the author would most likely agree that admitting art status is a conferral feels uncomfortable because:
options: people prefer to believe their value judgments are discoveries rather than decisions they are responsible for
conferring art status requires expertise that ordinary beholders lack
the marketplace has made all aesthetic judgments financially motivated
objects genuinely possess an intrinsic property of being art that we are reluctant to deny
questions.explanation: The fourth paragraph states "we want our judgments of value to be findings rather than choices" and that admitting conferral means accepting "responsibility for the canon" — option A. Option B introduces an expertise requirement the author never raises; the verdict is cultural, not credentialed. Option C overreaches: commerce appears only to refute the functional defense, not to claim all judgments are money-driven. Option D directly contradicts the author's view that art is not an intrinsic property of objects — the very comforting belief he is dismantling. A names the stated psychological motive.

questions.stem: A critic argues that street graffiti, once dismissed as vandalism, is now "truly art" because it always possessed aesthetic qualities that society failed to recognize. How would the author most likely respond?
options: By agreeing fully, since the author holds that art is an intrinsic property awaiting recognition
By objecting that the critic frames a cultural conferral as the detection of a pre-existing property
By insisting graffiti cannot be art because it once served a vandalistic function
By concluding that the change proves all aesthetic categories are arbitrary
questions.explanation: The author argues that calling something art is "an act of conferral, not detection," and resists treating verdicts as "findings." The critic's phrase "always possessed... qualities that society failed to recognize" is exactly the detection framing the author rejects — option B. Option A attributes to the author the intrinsic-property view he denies. Option C applies the functional defense the author already demolished (a use does not disqualify art). Option D overstates: the author explicitly says verdicts are "not arbitrary" and answer to skill and tradition. B matches the author's conferral-versus-detection distinction.

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most WEAKEN the author's claim that the art-craft boundary is "drawn by hands, not found in nature"?
options: A survey shows that museum curators across cultures disagree sharply about which objects qualify as art
Identical handmade vases are valued differently depending on whether a gallery or a kitchen displays them
A measurable, culture-independent physical signature reliably marks objects judged to be art and is absent for objects judged to be craft
Historical records confirm that the category of "fine arts" emerged only in the eighteenth century
questions.explanation: The author claims art status is conferred by culture, not an objective property of things. A universal, culture-independent physical marker (option C) would suggest the distinction tracks a real, found property — directly undercutting the thesis. Option A strengthens the author by showing variable, disagreeing verdicts. Option B also supports him: same object, different status by context. Option D restates the author's own historical evidence and reinforces contingency. Only C introduces a fixed, non-cultural basis for the line, which is precisely what the author denies; thus it is the strongest potential weakener.

title: The Weight of What We Keep
text:
We are urged, almost as a moral reflex, never to forget. Memorials rise where atrocities occurred; survivors are exhorted to testify; nations enshrine remembrance in statute and ceremony. The injunction has acquired the force of an axiom: to forget is to betray, to remember is to honor. Yet the elevation of memory into an unconditional duty deserves more scrutiny than it usually receives, for it rests on assumptions about the past, and about the self, that do not bear examination as well as we might wish.

Consider first that memory is not a faithful archive but a reconstruction, perpetually revised by the needs of the present. What a community chooses to remember is never the whole of what occurred; it is a selection, and every selection is also an erasure. The monument that commemorates the fallen soldier may quietly forget the deserter, the conscript, the civilian whose death served no narrative. To insist on remembrance, then, is not simply to oppose forgetting but to license a particular forgetting—one that wears the mask of fidelity. The pious phrase "lest we forget" can conceal a robust apparatus of selective amnesia.

There is, moreover, a cruelty in the demand. To require the wounded to keep their wounds open, to make of suffering a permanent civic exhibit, is to subordinate the living to the dead. Forgetting, here, is not negligence but mercy—the slow, necessary work by which a person, or a people, becomes capable of a future not wholly determined by its injuries. The amnesty that follows a civil war, the quiet agreement not to relitigate every grievance, is sometimes the only foundation on which a shared life can be rebuilt. He who forgives, after all, agrees in some measure to forget.

And yet I would not have the reader conclude that forgetting is therefore to be preferred. That would be to commit, in reverse, the very error I have been diagnosing—to convert a contingent good into an absolute. The forgetting that heals and the forgetting that conceals are not the same, though they are easily confused, and the difficulty lies precisely in telling them apart. A society that forgets the machinery of its own injustice does not heal; it merely prepares to repeat itself. The perpetrator's interest in oblivion and the victim's need for peace can issue in the same demand—"let it go"—while meaning entirely opposite things.

What follows is not a rule but a discipline of judgment. We are obliged neither to remember everything nor to forget at will, but to ask, of each act of remembrance and each act of release, whom it serves and to what end. Memory pursued as an end in itself can ossify into grievance, a wound cultivated for its own sake; forgetting embraced as relief can become complicity. The duty, if there is one, is not to the past as such but to the future the past makes possible—and that future is sometimes served by remembering, sometimes by letting go.

This is an unsatisfying conclusion, I concede, for it offers no formula. Those who crave a maxim will be disappointed. But the demand for a maxim is itself part of the problem, an attempt to be relieved of the labor of discernment. To remember well is not to remember much; it is to remember rightly, and what is right cannot be settled in advance. The past does not issue its own instructions. We must decide what to do with it, and we cannot be excused from deciding.

questions.stem: Which of the following best captures the central thesis of the passage?
options: Forgetting is generally more humane than remembering, because it frees the living from the tyranny of the dead.
Neither remembrance nor forgetting is an unconditional duty; each must be judged by whom it serves and what future it makes possible.
The duty to remember should always override the desire to forget, since forgetting injustice invites its repetition.
Because memory is an unreliable reconstruction, the moral injunction to remember rests on an illusion and should be abandoned.
questions.explanation: The author refuses to make either memory or forgetting absolute, concluding the duty is 'not to the past as such but to the future the past makes possible' and that one must ask 'whom it serves and to what end.' B captures this conditional, judgment-based stance. A is the reverse error the author explicitly warns against ('convert a contingent good into an absolute'). C states a one-sided position the author critiques in paragraph one and treats as only sometimes true. D overreaches: the author calls memory a reconstruction but never says the injunction is wholly illusory or should be 'abandoned'—forgetting injustice 'prepares to repeat itself.'

questions.stem: The author's overall tone toward the popular injunction 'never to forget' can best be described as:
options: Dismissive contempt for a slogan the author regards as wholly empty.
Critical scrutiny tempered by acknowledgment that remembering can be genuinely necessary.
Wholehearted endorsement qualified only by minor practical reservations.
Detached neutrality that declines to take any evaluative position.
questions.explanation: The author subjects the injunction to 'more scrutiny than it usually receives' yet affirms that a society forgetting its injustice 'prepares to repeat itself,' so the critique is tempered—matching B. A is too extreme; the author finds genuine value in remembrance, not emptiness. C reverses the emphasis: scrutiny dominates, not endorsement. D fails because the author plainly evaluates throughout ('there is a cruelty in the demand,' 'we cannot be excused from deciding'), taking definite positions rather than remaining neutral.

questions.stem: What is the primary function, within the argument, of the sentence: 'The perpetrator's interest in oblivion and the victim's need for peace can issue in the same demand—"let it go"—while meaning entirely opposite things'?
options: To prove that perpetrators are typically the ones who call for forgetting.
To illustrate why distinguishing healing forgetfulness from concealing forgetfulness is difficult yet essential.
To concede that the victim's desire for peace is morally indistinguishable from the perpetrator's self-interest.
To establish that all calls to 'let it go' are forms of complicity.
questions.explanation: The sentence appears amid the claim that the two kinds of forgetting 'are easily confused, and the difficulty lies precisely in telling them apart'; the example shows identical language masking opposite intents, supporting B. A misreads it as an empirical claim about who usually calls for forgetting. C inverts the point—the author insists the two 'mean entirely opposite things,' not that they are indistinguishable in worth. D is too sweeping; the author allows that some forgetting 'heals' and that forgiveness 'agrees in some measure to forget,' so not all such calls are complicity.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that the author would most likely regard a demand for a clear 'rule' governing when to remember and when to forget as:
options: A reasonable request that the author regrets being unable to fully satisfy.
An evasion of the very discernment the situation requires.
Achievable in principle but premature given current disagreement.
Necessary, since without a rule people will default to self-serving forgetting.
questions.explanation: The author states 'the demand for a maxim is itself part of the problem, an attempt to be relieved of the labor of discernment,' directly matching B. A misreads the concession: he calls the conclusion 'unsatisfying' but argues a rule is undesirable, not that he regrets lacking one. C wrongly implies a rule is achievable later, but the author says 'what is right cannot be settled in advance.' D treats a rule as necessary, whereas the author's whole point is that judgment, not a rule, is the remedy.

questions.stem: A city council debates removing a long-standing statue honoring a figure now widely seen as having enabled an injustice. Based on the author's reasoning, the council should primarily ask:
options: Whether the statue is historically accurate in every detail it depicts.
Whether keeping or removing the statue better serves the future the community is trying to build.
Whether more residents favor remembrance than favor forgetting.
Whether removing the statue would constitute a betrayal of the past.
questions.explanation: The author's discipline of judgment asks 'of each act of remembrance and each act of release, whom it serves and to what end,' orienting decisions toward 'the future the past makes possible'—exactly B. A fixates on factual accuracy, which the author never makes the criterion. C reduces the matter to a popularity count, but the author offers a normative test of ends served, not a vote. D smuggles in the 'forgetting is betrayal' axiom the author explicitly challenges as concealing 'selective amnesia,' so it contradicts his framework.

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most WEAKEN the author's claim that forgetting can be 'the slow, necessary work by which a people becomes capable of a future not wholly determined by its injuries'?
options: Some communities that erect elaborate memorials nonetheless flourish economically.
Post-conflict societies that suppressed memory of past atrocities reliably descended into renewed violence, while those that publicly remembered achieved durable peace.
Individuals often misremember the details of traumatic events they personally witnessed.
Many amnesties after civil wars were negotiated primarily to protect powerful perpetrators.
questions.explanation: The author credits forgetting with enabling a peaceful shared future; B contradicts this by showing that remembering, not forgetting, produces durable peace while suppression yields renewed violence, undercutting the causal claim. A is irrelevant—economic flourishing says nothing about whether forgetting heals. C concerns memory's accuracy, which the author already grants, and does not bear on forgetting's healing role. D actually supports the author's separate warning that the 'perpetrator's interest in oblivion' can masquerade as peace, rather than weakening the healing claim.

title: The Faithful Traitor
text:
The Italian pun "traduttore, traditore"—translator, traitor—has long served as a convenient verdict on the impossibility of translation. To carry a text across the gap between languages, the saying implies, is inevitably to betray it. Yet the proverb conceals more than it reveals, for it presumes that fidelity is a property a translation either possesses or lacks, as though the original were a fixed quantity to be preserved or squandered. This assumption deserves scrutiny.

Consider what it would mean to be faithful to a poem. A reader who demands that the translation reproduce the original's literal sense will find that meaning alone, transferred word for word, often yields prose that is dead on the page—accurate yet inert, conveying what the lines say while losing what they do. A reader who instead prizes the music of the verse, its rhythm and resonance, may accept liberties with sense in order to keep the poem alive as a poem. These two readers are not disagreeing about how well a given translation succeeds; they are disagreeing about what success would even consist in. Fidelity, it turns out, is not one standard but several, and they frequently pull against one another.

This is why the charge of betrayal so often misfires. The translator who sacrifices literal accuracy to preserve a poem's force has not failed to be faithful; she has been faithful to a different aspect of the work. Every translation is, in this sense, an argument—a claim about what in the original most deserves to survive. To translate is to interpret, and to interpret is to choose. The notion of a perfectly faithful translation, one that betrays nothing, is therefore incoherent, because it imagines a translation answerable to all dimensions of the original at once, when those dimensions cannot all be honored simultaneously.

One might object that this reasoning licenses anything, that if every choice is interpretation then no translation can be judged wrong. But the conclusion does not follow. To say that fidelity is plural is not to say it is arbitrary. A translation can still misread its source, mistake irony for sincerity, flatten a register the original kept taut. What changes is the basis of the judgment: we no longer ask whether a translation matches some impossible totality, but whether its interpretation is defensible—whether the reading it embodies can be sustained by the text it claims to render. A reckless translation betrays the original; a careful one converses with it.

Here a subtler point emerges. If translation is interpretation, then the original itself is never simply "given." We tend to speak as if the source text were stable and the translation derivative, but the act of translating exposes ambiguities a monolingual reader passes over without noticing. The translator must decide what a pronoun refers to, whether a phrase is bitter or tender, where an author's emphasis falls—decisions the original leaves open. In rendering these choices explicit, the translation does not merely copy the original; it completes a reading of it that the original alone could never settle. The translated text is thus not a diminished copy but a distinct work, bearing the marks of a particular encounter.

Perhaps, then, the proverb should be inverted. The faithful translator is precisely the one who betrays—who refuses the fantasy of neutral transfer and accepts the responsibility of choosing. Betrayal, understood this way, is not the failure of translation but its condition. What we should ask of a translation is not that it commit no treason, but that its treasons be honest ones.

questions.stem: Which of the following best captures the central thesis of the passage?
options: Because no translation can preserve every aspect of an original, all translations are equally valid interpretations.
Translation is necessarily interpretive, so fidelity should be judged by whether a translation's chosen reading is defensible rather than by whether it betrays nothing.
Literal accuracy is the only legitimate standard of fidelity, and translators who abandon it betray their sources.
The proverb 'translator, traitor' is correct because carrying a text across languages always destroys its original meaning.
questions.explanation: The passage argues that translation is interpretation, that fidelity is plural rather than singular, and that the proper question is whether a translation's interpretation is 'defensible.' Option B captures both moves. Option A reverses the author, who explicitly denies that plurality makes judgment 'arbitrary' or every translation valid ('the conclusion does not follow'). Option C states the literal-accuracy view the author critiques as yielding prose 'dead on the page.' Option D endorses the proverb's verdict of betrayal-as-failure, whereas the author reinterprets betrayal as translation's honest 'condition,' not its destruction. Only B reflects the author's actual stance.

questions.stem: The author's overall attitude toward the proverb 'traduttore, traditore' is best described as:
options: Dismissive, regarding it as wholly meaningless and unworthy of discussion.
Reverent, treating it as the definitive truth about translation.
Critical yet constructive, rejecting its surface verdict while repurposing its central image of betrayal.
Indifferent, since the author considers the question of fidelity unanswerable.
questions.explanation: The author calls the proverb a verdict that 'conceals more than it reveals' and ultimately proposes to 'invert' it, recasting betrayal as translation's 'condition'—criticism paired with reuse, hence 'critical yet constructive.' Option A is too extreme: the author engages the proverb seriously across the essay, not as meaningless. Option B reverses the stance; the author challenges, not venerates, it. Option D contradicts the closing claim that we can and should ask whether a translation's 'treasons be honest ones,' which presupposes the question is answerable. Only C matches the dual gesture of rejection and repurposing.

questions.stem: The author introduces the two hypothetical readers of a poem (one prizing literal sense, one prizing music) primarily in order to:
options: Demonstrate that most disagreements about translation stem from carelessness rather than principle.
Establish that fidelity is not a single standard but comprises competing standards that cannot all be satisfied at once.
Prove that the musical qualities of verse always matter more than literal meaning.
Show that readers who disagree about a translation cannot reason with one another.
questions.explanation: Immediately after the example, the author concludes that the readers 'are disagreeing about what success would even consist in' and that 'fidelity... is not one standard but several.' The two readers ground that claim. Option A misreads the disagreement as carelessness; the author frames it as a principled clash of standards. Option C overstates: the author presents music and sense as competing legitimate priorities, never ranking one above the other. Option D is unsupported—the author later argues judgments remain possible and 'defensible,' implying disagreement can be reasoned about. Only B states the example's actual function.

questions.stem: Which of the following claims, if true, would most directly UNDERMINE the author's argument?
options: Different translators of the same poem frequently produce markedly different versions.
There exists a method that allows every dimension of an original text to be preserved simultaneously without loss.
Monolingual readers sometimes notice ambiguities in a text on their own.
Some translations clearly mistake irony for sincerity.
questions.explanation: The author's core claim is that 'those dimensions cannot all be honored simultaneously,' which makes perfect fidelity 'incoherent' and translation necessarily interpretive. A method preserving every dimension at once (B) would dissolve the very tension the argument rests on, so it is the strongest threat. Option A actually supports the author by illustrating that translation involves interpretive choice. Option C concedes a minor point but doesn't touch the central claim that translating forces choices the original leaves open. Option D restates something the author affirms—that translations can misread—so it strengthens rather than weakens. Only B strikes the load-bearing premise.

questions.stem: A museum hires two conservators to restore a faded mural. One repaints the colors to their original vividness, accepting some guesswork about lost details; the other preserves only what survives, leaving gaps visible. Based on the passage's reasoning, the author would most likely conclude that:
options: The second conservator is faithful and the first is a traitor, since only literal preservation counts as fidelity.
Neither approach can be judged, because both are merely interpretations of the mural.
Each conservator is faithful to a different aspect of the mural, and each embodies an interpretation that can still be judged by whether its choices can be sustained by the work.
The first conservator's work should be preferred because vividness keeps the mural alive.
questions.explanation: The author holds that fidelity is plural—one can be 'faithful to a different aspect of the work'—while insisting judgment remains possible because an interpretation must be 'defensible,' able to be 'sustained by the text.' Option C maps both halves onto the conservation case. Option A imports the literal-only standard the author rejects. Option B commits the 'licenses anything' error the author explicitly refutes ('the conclusion does not follow'). Option D mirrors the poem example, but the author never ranks vividness/music above preservation as a general rule; he treats them as competing legitimate priorities. Only C applies the author's dual commitment: plural fidelity plus accountable judgment.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that, in the act of translating, the original text is 'never simply given.' Which scenario best illustrates this idea?
options: A translator works faster than expected because the source language closely resembles the target language.
A translator must decide whether an ambiguous line is sarcastic or sincere, a question the source text leaves unresolved, and the translation thereby fixes a reading the original never settled.
A publisher releases two translations of a novel and lets readers choose between them.
A translator consults a dictionary to find the most common meaning of an unfamiliar word.
questions.explanation: The author argues that translating 'exposes ambiguities a monolingual reader passes over' and forces the translator to settle questions—'whether a phrase is bitter or tender'—that the original 'leaves open,' so the translation 'completes a reading.' Option B is exactly this: an ambiguity the source does not resolve, made explicit by the translator. Option A concerns linguistic ease, irrelevant to the original's openness. Option C is about reception and choice among finished products, not the interpretive indeterminacy within a source. Option D involves resolving mere vocabulary, not the structural ambiguity over meaning the author means. Only B captures the original's underdetermined, 'not given' character.

title: The Eloquence of Nothing
text:
A painting of a storm depicts a storm; a novel about grief names the grief and the griever. But a symphony, stripped of any text or program, depicts nothing at all. Its phrases do not refer; its cadences point to no object in the world. And yet listeners weep at the slow movement of a quartet they could not paraphrase if their lives depended on it. This is the standing puzzle of instrumental music: it appears to be the most abstract of the arts and is, by common report, the most directly moving. How can what represents nothing affect us so much?

One tempting answer holds that music is, after all, a secret language of the emotions—that a falling minor third just is sadness, encoded. On this view the puzzle dissolves: music moves us because it depicts feelings the way a portrait depicts a face. But the analogy strains under pressure. A portrait of a sad man does not make us sad; it makes us recognize sadness. Music, by contrast, seems to install the feeling in us rather than present it for inspection. If a passage merely labeled grief, we could note the label and remain unmoved, as we do before a dictionary entry. We are not unmoved. So whatever music does, it is not, or not merely, naming.

A second answer, more austere, concedes that music represents nothing and concludes that it therefore expresses nothing—that the tears are a confusion, a sentimental projection onto patterns of sound that are, in themselves, as meaningless as wallpaper. I find this too quick. It mistakes the absence of reference for the absence of significance. A face can be expressive without being a sign of anything; a gesture can carry feeling without standing for it. To deny music meaning because it lacks reference is to assume that all meaning is reference, which is precisely the assumption a serious account of music ought to question.

Here is the position I want to defend, tentatively. Music moves us not by representing emotions but by enacting their dynamic form—the swelling and subsiding, the tension and release, the hesitation and arrival that emotions share with melodies. Grief is not a static fact; it is a motion of the soul, and a phrase that strains upward and then sinks does not describe that motion but rehearses it, drawing the listener's own attention through the same arc. We are moved because we are, briefly, moving in the music's way.

Yet I should not overstate the case. This account explains why music can be expressive without being representational, but it does not show that every moving passage works by mirroring an emotion's shape. Some music thrills us by sheer ingenuity—a fugue's architecture, a modulation's wit—and to call our delight there a rehearsed feeling is to flatten it. The dynamic-form theory may be the deepest part of the story without being the whole of it. Indeed, the most powerful works likely fuse the two: a structure admired by the intellect that simultaneously carries the body through a shape it knows from within.

What I would resist, finally, is the demand that music justify its power by translation—that we vindicate the tears only by saying what they are "about." Perhaps the lesson of instrumental music is the reverse: that some of the most consequential things we undergo are not about anything, and that our craving to convert every experience into a statement is itself a kind of deafness. The symphony represents nothing, and in representing nothing it may show us something we are otherwise too literal to see.

questions.stem: The central thesis the author advances is that instrumental music moves us primarily because it:
options: functions as a coded language in which musical figures directly name specific emotions
enacts the dynamic shape that emotions and melodies share, leading listeners to move through the same arc
is meaningless in itself, so any emotion we feel is a sentimental projection
can always be translated into a statement about what the feeling is 'about'
questions.explanation: The fourth paragraph announces the author's defended position: music moves us 'not by representing emotions but by enacting their dynamic form,' so we are moved because 'we are, briefly, moving in the music's way' (B). Option A is the 'secret language' view the author says 'strains under pressure.' Option C is the 'austere' answer the author calls 'too quick' for mistaking absence of reference for absence of significance. Option D inverts the closing paragraph, where the author explicitly resists 'the demand that music justify its power by translation.' Both A and C are positions canvassed and refuted, making them tempting but wrong.

questions.stem: The author's overall attitude toward the dynamic-form theory is best described as:
options: unreservedly certain that it fully explains music's power
committed but qualified, presenting it as deep yet incomplete
dismissive, treating it as one more failed account
neutral, declining to favor any of the theories
questions.explanation: The author introduces the theory 'tentatively,' defends it, then says 'I should not overstate the case,' conceding it 'does not show that every moving passage' works this way and may be 'the deepest part of the story without being the whole of it' (B). Option A is too strong: the author expressly warns against overstating. Option C reverses the stance; the author defends this view rather than dismissing it, as he does the other two. Option D fails because the author clearly endorses this position over the rivals, calling them 'too quick' and strained. The qualification is genuine but is not neutrality.

questions.stem: The author offers the claim that a portrait of a sad man 'does not make us sad; it makes us recognize sadness' chiefly in order to:
options: prove that visual art is incapable of moving viewers emotionally
show that music, unlike the portrait, installs feeling rather than merely presenting it
establish that all art works by naming the emotions it depicts
concede that music and painting move us in identical ways
questions.explanation: The portrait example is deployed to break the 'secret language' analogy: a portrait makes us 'recognize' sadness, whereas music 'seems to install the feeling in us rather than present it for inspection' (B). The contrast shows naming cannot be all music does. Option A overreaches; the author claims only that this portrait prompts recognition, not that painting cannot move us. Option C is the very assumption the author is undermining. Option D is the opposite of the point, since the example is drawn precisely to distinguish music's effect from the portrait's. The claim functions as a counterexample, not a concession.

questions.stem: Which assumption is most essential to the author's critique of the 'austere' second answer?
options: Meaning and reference are not the same thing, so something can mean without referring
All genuine meaning must ultimately be reducible to reference to objects
Music is in fact a representational art that depicts hidden objects
Emotional responses to art are always reliable guides to artistic value
questions.explanation: The author rejects the austere view because it 'mistakes the absence of reference for the absence of significance,' citing a face that is 'expressive without being a sign of anything.' This rests on separating meaning from reference (A): to deny music meaning for lacking reference 'is to assume that all meaning is reference,' which the author treats as a mistaken assumption. Option B is exactly the assumption the author attributes to the opponent and rejects. Option C contradicts the author, who repeatedly grants that music 'represents nothing.' Option D about reliability of responses is never used as a premise; the critique turns on the meaning/reference distinction.

questions.stem: A listener reports being thrilled by the intricate counterpoint of a fugue, delighting in how its voices interlock. According to the passage, this response:
options: is fully explained by the dynamic-form theory as a rehearsed emotion
shows that music has no meaning apart from intellectual puzzle-solving
is an example the author cites as not reducible to mirroring an emotion's shape
refutes the author's entire account of why music moves us
questions.explanation: In the fifth paragraph the author concedes that 'some music thrills us by sheer ingenuity—a fugue's architecture, a modulation's wit—and to call our delight there a rehearsed feeling is to flatten it' (C). Such delight is precisely what the dynamic-form theory does NOT fully cover. Option A reverses this concession. Option B overgeneralizes; the author still treats music as meaningful and imagines structure fused with felt shape. Option D is too strong: the author treats the fugue as a limit on the theory's scope, not a refutation, calling dynamic form 'the deepest part of the story without being the whole.'

questions.stem: Suppose researchers find that listeners across many cultures consistently match rising, accelerating passages to feelings of mounting anticipation. According to the passage's reasoning, this finding would most directly:
options: weaken the author's view by showing music is a literal coded language
strengthen the author's claim that music enacts the dynamic form shared by emotions
be irrelevant, since the author denies music has any connection to emotion
support the austere view that emotional responses are mere projection
questions.explanation: The author claims emotions share with melodies 'the swelling and subsiding, the tension and release,' so a phrase 'straining upward' rehearses an emotion's motion. Cross-cultural matching of rising, accelerating sound to mounting anticipation tracks that shared dynamic shape, supporting the thesis (B). Option A misreads the data as vindicating the 'coded language' view, but matching contours is about dynamic form, not fixed labels for discrete emotions. Option C is false: the author affirms a deep link between music and emotion. Option D is backwards, since consistent, predictable matching across cultures undercuts the 'mere projection' charge rather than supporting it, projection being idiosyncratic.

title: The Author's Shadow
text:
When a critic declares that the author is dead, the claim is rarely meant biographically. It is a wager about where meaning resides. On one account, a text is a sealed message: the writer encodes an intention, the reader decodes it, and interpretation succeeds insofar as it recovers what the writer meant to say. On the rival account, the moment a work enters the world it becomes an orphan, severed from its parent's purposes, free to be reanimated by every reader who encounters it. The author's biography, letters, and stated aims become, at most, one more document to be interpreted, with no special authority over the words on the page.

The seductive power of the second view lies in its honesty about reading as it actually happens. We routinely find in poems meanings their makers never consciously entertained, and we do not regard this as a mistake. A novel composed to flatter an empire can be read, generations later, as an indictment of it; the irony is not smuggled in by perverse readers but disclosed by history. To insist that such readings are illegitimate unless ratified by the dead writer's intention is to make the richest acts of criticism impossible.

Yet the doctrine, pressed to its limit, dissolves into incoherence. If intention is irrelevant, why do we treat a typographical error differently from a deliberate coinage? Why does the discovery that a passage was meant ironically change how we weigh it, rather than counting as merely one reading among equals? The very practice of careful reading presupposes that we are tracking something the words were used to do, and "use" is unintelligible without a user. A text cut entirely loose from any imagined intending mind is not liberated; it is reduced to ink, a Rorschach blot onto which we project whatever we please.

I want to suggest that the dispute trades on a false dichotomy. The relevant intention need not be the historical author's private psychology, recoverable through biography, nor need it be discarded. What constrains interpretation is the intention the text itself warrants—the purpose a competent reader must attribute to its words to make sense of them as language at all. This intention is public, lodged in conventions of genre and usage, not locked in a vanished skull. We reconstruct it as we reconstruct the meaning of an overheard remark: not by séance, but by asking what someone, situated thus, using these words, could coherently have been doing.

On this view, the author is neither sovereign nor dead but demoted to the status of a hypothesis—indispensable, yet revisable. When new evidence about a writer's circumstances illuminates a passage, we admit it gladly, because it sharpens our hypothesis about what the words were doing. When such evidence merely records a wish the text failed to realize, we set it aside, because intention that does not make it onto the page commands no authority. The page is the court of appeal; the author is a witness, sometimes expert, sometimes unreliable, never the judge.

This is less a compromise than a clarification. It explains why we can be surprised by a text, taught by it things its writer did not know, while still being answerable to it rather than to our own fancy. The death of the author, properly understood, is not the abolition of intention but the relocation of it—from the person who wrote to the work that was written. The corpse, it turns out, was never the author's. It was a caricature of authorship that no careful reader ever truly held.

questions.stem: Which of the following best captures the central thesis of the passage?
options: Authorial intention is irrelevant to interpretation, since texts become orphans the moment they are published.
The debate over authorial intention rests on a false dichotomy; meaning is governed by an intention the text itself warrants rather than by the writer's private psychology.
Careful readers should always recover what the historical author consciously meant, using biography and letters as decisive evidence.
Because readers inevitably find meanings authors never intended, all interpretations of a text are equally valid.
questions.explanation: The author explicitly frames the dispute as trading on a 'false dichotomy' and proposes that interpretation is constrained by 'the intention the text itself warrants,' a public intention lodged in conventions rather than the historical author's psychology. B states exactly this. A and C each endorse one pole the author rejects: A is the view that 'dissolves into incoherence'; C is the 'sealed message' view that would make the richest criticism impossible. D ('all interpretations equally valid') is the relativism the author attacks with the 'Rorschach blot' image. Each distractor captures a position the passage cites only to qualify or refute, not the synthesizing thesis.

questions.stem: The author's overall attitude toward the slogan "the author is dead" can best be described as:
options: Dismissive, treating it as a fashionable error with no insight to offer.
Reverent, accepting it as the definitive account of how meaning works.
Qualified endorsement, salvaging a defensible core while rejecting its extreme form.
Indifferent, presenting both sides without taking any position.
questions.explanation: The author neither rejects nor embraces the slogan wholesale. He grants its 'seductive power' and 'honesty about reading,' yet shows it 'dissolves into incoherence' when 'pressed to its limit,' finally redefining it as 'the relocation' of intention rather than its 'abolition.' That is qualified endorsement. A is too dismissive—he credits genuine insight. B is too reverent—he calls the extreme version incoherent. D is wrong because the closing paragraphs stake out a clear stance ('I want to suggest,' 'On this view'). The tone is evaluative and committed, ruling out indifference.

questions.stem: The author distinguishes a typographical error from a deliberate coinage (paragraph 3) primarily in order to:
options: Show that even careful readers frequently misread the texts they encounter.
Demonstrate that the strict intentionalist 'sealed message' view cannot account for ordinary reading.
Argue that interpretation is impossible without recovering the writer's biography.
Reveal that the intention-irrelevant view cannot explain a distinction readers actually make.
questions.explanation: The error/coinage contrast appears in the paragraph beginning 'Yet the doctrine, pressed to its limit, dissolves into incoherence,' which targets the view that 'intention is irrelevant.' The point is that this view cannot explain why we treat the two differently—a distinction grounded in what the words were 'used to do.' So D names the correct target. B reverses the target: this paragraph attacks the anti-intention view, not strict intentionalism (treated in paragraph 2). A and C distort the function: the example concerns the role of intention, not reader fallibility (A) or any claim that biography is required (C)—indeed the author later denies biography is necessary.

questions.stem: Based on the passage, the author would most likely regard a reading which finds anti-imperial irony in a novel written to flatter an empire as:
options: Legitimate, because such irony can be disclosed by history without being smuggled in by the reader.
Illegitimate, because the writer's flattering intention was never realized on the page.
Legitimate only if letters confirm the author secretly opposed the empire.
Indeterminate, since the passage holds that no text can support one reading over another.
questions.explanation: The passage offers this very example, saying the irony 'is not smuggled in by perverse readers but disclosed by history,' and insists that ruling such readings out would 'make the richest acts of criticism impossible.' That endorses A. B inverts the case—the author treats this reading as valid, not as a failed intention. C contradicts the thesis that intention is public and text-warranted, 'not locked in a vanished skull'; biographical confirmation is welcomed but not required. D states the relativism the author rejects via the 'Rorschach blot' and 'court of appeal' images, which hold that the page does constrain readings.

questions.stem: A scholar discovers a diary entry in which a poet writes, "I wanted my poem to convey serene hope," though the published poem is, by every competent reading, bleak and despairing. The passage's reasoning most strongly supports which response?
options: Reinterpret the poem as serene, since the diary records the author's genuine intention.
Treat the poem as having no determinate meaning, since author and text now conflict.
Set the diary aside as a wish the text failed to realize, letting the poem on the page govern.
Conclude that competent readers have erred and must defer to the poet's stated aim.
questions.explanation: The passage directly addresses this: when evidence 'merely records a wish the text failed to realize, we set it aside, because intention that does not make it onto the page commands no authority,' and 'the page is the court of appeal; the author is a witness... never the judge.' That is exactly C. A and D make the author sovereign, the precise position the passage demotes. B misapplies the framework—the author never says conflict yields indeterminacy; the public, text-warranted intention still fixes a bleak meaning. C alone tracks the rule that unrealized intention carries no authority.

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most WEAKEN the author's claim that interpretation must track "the intention the text itself warrants"?
options: Readers sometimes discover meanings the writer never consciously entertained.
Knowledge of a writer's historical circumstances can sharpen a reading of a passage.
Competent readers reliably converge on a coherent reading of an overheard remark without knowing the speaker.
Skilled readers, fully sharing the same conventions of genre and usage, systematically reach contradictory readings of the same text that no appeal to usage or genre can adjudicate.
questions.explanation: The author's view depends on a public intention 'lodged in conventions of genre and usage' that constrains reading the way an 'overheard remark' is understood. If equally skilled readers, fully sharing those conventions, still reach irreconcilable readings that usage and genre cannot adjudicate (D), then the supposed text-warranted intention fails to constrain anything—directly undercutting the thesis. A is already embraced by the author as compatible with his view (surprise without 'fancy'). B supports him: he says such evidence 'sharpens our hypothesis.' C echoes his own overheard-remark analogy, reinforcing rather than weakening the position. Only D removes the constraint the argument requires.

title: The Honest Chronicler's Dilemma
text:
We tend to assume that the historian's first duty is to be truthful, and that truthfulness, in turn, demands the suppression of self. The ideal practitioner, on this view, recedes behind the documents, permitting the past to speak in its own voice. Yet the more closely one examines what historians actually do, the more this ideal dissolves into something stranger and less consoling. No archive arranges itself. The very act of deciding which letter to transcribe, which ledger to ignore, which silence to interpret as meaningful, is already an act of authorship. The past does not have a voice; it has only traces, and traces require a reader to become testimony.

This is not the same as the fashionable claim that all accounts are equally fictions, that the chronicler of a famine and the chronicler of a banquet are merely telling different stories with no purchase on what happened. Such relativism mistakes the difficulty of objectivity for its impossibility, and in doing so flatters the very subjectivity it claims to expose. There remains a meaningful difference between an account constrained by evidence and one indifferent to it. A historian who fabricates a treaty has not exercised perspective; she has lied. The interpretive freedom that selection grants is real, but it operates within walls the evidence builds, and those walls are not infinitely elastic.

Where, then, does objectivity live, if not in the absence of the historian? I would locate it not in a vanished standpoint but in a discipline of method—in the willingness to seek out the document that embarrasses one's thesis, to state the rival reading fairly before dismantling it, to mark the line between what the sources license and what one merely suspects. Objectivity, so understood, is not a view from nowhere but a practiced honesty about the view from somewhere. It is procedural rather than positional, an ethic rather than a vantage.

This reframing carries a cost that its defenders rarely admit. If objectivity is a method, then it can never be fully verified from the finished page. The reader encounters only the historian's conclusions, not the temptations she resisted nor the counter-evidence she weighed and set aside. We are asked, in effect, to trust a labor we cannot witness. The polished narrative, smooth and authoritative, may conceal exactly the same shortcuts as the dishonest one; indeed, the more elegant the prose, the more thoroughly the seams of judgment are hidden. Eloquence and rigor are not enemies, but neither are they reliably allies.

Some would resolve this by demanding that historians foreground their own situatedness—announcing their commitments so that readers may discount for them. I am sympathetic, but only to a point. A declaration of bias is not the same as a correction for it, and confession can become its own performance, a way of purchasing credibility cheaply while changing nothing in the underlying work. The historian who loudly proclaims her partiality may be no more trustworthy than the one who quietly practices restraint; she has merely relocated the appeal to trust.

In the end the discipline rests on something it cannot guarantee: the character of the person doing it. We have grown uncomfortable with such language, preferring procedures we can audit to virtues we must take on faith. But the audit is incomplete, and always will be. The chronicle that deserves our belief is written by someone who would have told us what she found even if it ruined her argument—and that disposition, finally, no method can manufacture and no preface can prove.

questions.stem: Which of the following best captures the central thesis of the passage?
options: Historical narratives are fictions whose claims to truth cannot be meaningfully ranked against one another.
Historians achieve objectivity precisely when they succeed in removing their own perspective from the account.
Objectivity in history is best understood as a disciplined method that ultimately depends on the historian's character, which no procedure can fully verify.
The chief threat to honest history is eloquent prose, which conceals the shortcuts that plain writing would reveal.
questions.explanation: The passage redefines objectivity as 'procedural rather than positional,' an 'ethic,' then argues this method 'can never be fully verified' and finally 'rests on... the character of the person doing it' that 'no method can manufacture.' Option C captures both the reframing and the concluding turn. Option A is the relativism the author explicitly rejects as mistaking difficulty for impossibility. Option B is the 'absence of self' ideal the author says 'dissolves.' Option D overstates a supporting observation about eloquence into the main point; the author calls eloquence and rigor neither enemies nor reliable allies, not the chief threat.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward historians who openly announce their biases is best described as:
options: wholly dismissive, treating such declarations as deliberate deceptions
partially sympathetic but skeptical that disclosure substitutes for genuine correction
enthusiastically supportive, viewing disclosure as the solution to the verification problem
indifferent, regarding the practice as having no bearing on trustworthiness
questions.explanation: The author writes 'I am sympathetic, but only to a point,' then warns that 'a declaration of bias is not the same as a correction for it' and can become 'its own performance.' That is qualified sympathy plus skepticism—option B. Option A is too harsh: 'sympathetic' rules out wholesale dismissal, and the author says such historians 'may be no more trustworthy,' not that they are deceivers. Option C reverses the author, who denies disclosure solves the problem. Option D is wrong because the author engages the practice substantively rather than treating it as irrelevant; he argues it relocates rather than removes the appeal to trust.

questions.stem: The author distinguishes his position from 'relativism' primarily in order to:
options: concede that fabrication and interpretation are ultimately indistinguishable
establish that interpretive freedom operates within real constraints set by evidence
argue that the chronicler of a famine is more reliable than the chronicler of a banquet
demonstrate that objectivity requires the complete elimination of the historian's judgment
questions.explanation: After granting that selection is 'an act of authorship,' the author guards against the inference that anything goes: relativism 'mistakes the difficulty of objectivity for its impossibility,' and interpretive freedom 'operates within walls the evidence builds.' The function is to preserve evidentiary constraint—option B. Option A reverses him: he insists fabrication is 'a lie,' clearly distinct from interpretation. Option C misreads the famine/banquet example, which illustrates relativism's flattening, not a ranking of topics. Option D contradicts his whole project: he relocates objectivity into method, explicitly denying it needs the historian's absence or the elimination of judgment.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that a 'polished narrative, smooth and authoritative' is potentially troubling because:
options: elegant prose is inherently less accurate than plain prose
its smoothness may hide the very judgments and omissions a reader would need to evaluate its rigor
an authoritative tone proves that the historian has suppressed counter-evidence
readers prefer eloquence to truth and therefore reward dishonest historians
questions.explanation: The author says the polished narrative 'may conceal exactly the same shortcuts as the dishonest one' and that 'the more elegant the prose, the more thoroughly the seams of judgment are hidden'—the danger is concealment of evaluable judgment, option B. Option A overgeneralizes: he explicitly says 'eloquence and rigor are not enemies,' so elegance is not inherently inaccurate. Option C is too strong—'may conceal' signals possibility, not proof, and nowhere does authoritative tone establish suppression. Option D invents a claim about reader preferences and rewards that the passage never makes; the concern is epistemic concealment, not audience taste.

questions.stem: A journal institutes a rule requiring every submitted history article to include an appended log of all sources the author consulted but chose not to cite, along with reasons. According to the author's reasoning, this reform would most likely be judged:
options: a complete solution, since it makes the historian's method fully auditable on the page
worthless, because any disclosure is merely a performance that changes nothing
a genuine but incomplete improvement, since it exposes more of the weighing yet still cannot certify the disposition behind it
misguided, because foregrounding sources increases bias rather than correcting it
questions.explanation: The author's verification problem is that readers see 'only the historian's conclusions, not the temptations she resisted nor the counter-evidence she weighed and set aside.' A log of set-aside sources directly surfaces that hidden weighing, so it helps—but the author insists 'the audit is incomplete, and always will be,' resting finally on character that 'no preface can prove.' Hence genuine but incomplete: option C. Option A claims full auditability the author denies. Option B overreads his skepticism of confession into total worthlessness, ignoring that he treats seeking the embarrassing document as a real virtue. Option D invents a bias-increasing claim found nowhere in the text.

questions.stem: Which scenario most closely parallels the author's view of how objectivity should be understood?
options: A judge whose impartiality is secured by knowing none of the facts of the case before ruling
A referee whose fairness consists in consistently applying the rules and acknowledging calls that favor the side he privately dislikes
A scientist who reports only the experiments that confirmed her hypothesis but writes in neutral language
A critic who declares his favorite author at the outset so readers can discount his praise
questions.explanation: The author locates objectivity in a 'discipline of method'—seeking the embarrassing document, stating the rival reading fairly, marking the limits of evidence—an 'ethic' practiced from a standpoint, not the absence of one. The referee in option B embodies this: fairness as consistent procedure plus acknowledging calls against his own preference, mirroring 'honesty about the view from somewhere.' Option A is the 'view from nowhere'/absence ideal the author rejects. Option C is the selective, eloquent dishonesty he warns against, since reporting only confirming experiments hides the weighing. Option D is the mere-confession move he calls insufficient, since declaring bias 'is not the same as a correction for it.'

title: The Burden of Becoming
text:
The vocabulary of authenticity has become so worn by ordinary speech that we forget how strange a demand it originally made. To be told to "be yourself" now sounds like the mildest of encouragements, a permission slip to relax. But in the existentialist tradition from which the idea descends, the injunction to be authentic was less a comfort than an indictment. It named a labor, not a state; a perpetual task, not a possession one could secure and set aside.

The confusion arises because we imagine the self as a hidden object, like a coin buried in sand, that authenticity merely uncovers. On this picture, beneath the crust of social expectation lies a "true self" with fixed preferences and native talents, and the only work required is excavation. Strip away the parental ambitions, the borrowed opinions, the performances we stage for others, and what remains will be the genuine article. Yet the existentialists I find most persuasive deny that any such buried coin exists. For them there is no self anterior to its choices, waiting to be found. The self is not discovered but made, and made continuously, through the commitments one undertakes and renews. Authenticity, then, cannot mean fidelity to a pre-given essence, because there is no essence to which one might be faithful.

If this is right, the popular slogan inverts the actual difficulty. "Be yourself" implies that the self is already there and that conformity alone obscures it. But the harder truth is that there is nothing already there—only a freedom that must keep choosing, and that remains answerable for whatever it chooses. What we evade when we hide behind convention is not some inner treasure but the vertigo of this responsibility. The crowd is consoling precisely because it relieves us of authorship. To do as "one" does, to want what "anyone" wants, is to borrow a self ready-made and thereby to disown the burden of having to fashion one.

This is why the demand is an indictment. Inauthenticity is not a failure to express a hidden nature; it is a flight from the labor of self-creation, a refusal dressed up as humility or realism. And here a complication must be admitted, for the account risks making authenticity sound like sheer self-invention, as if one could conjure a self from nothing by an act of will. That would be a caricature. We do not choose in a vacuum. We are thrown into a particular body, history, and situation we did not select, and authenticity consists not in escaping these conditions but in the manner of taking them up—owning them as the material of one's own project rather than as excuses that absolve one of choosing.

So the authentic person is not the one who has finally located her real self and now lives in accordance with it. She is the one who acknowledges that no such terminus exists, that the self is an ongoing achievement perpetually undone by the next decision, and who nonetheless does not flinch from the choosing. There is something almost paradoxical in this: the surest sign of authenticity may be the refusal to believe one has attained it. The moment we declare ourselves authentic, we have once again treated the self as a settled possession—and so betrayed the very freedom that the concept was meant to honor.

questions.stem: The author's central thesis is that authenticity is best understood as:
options: the lifelong process of recovering preferences and talents obscured by social conditioning
an ongoing task of self-creation undertaken in conditions one did not choose
the consistent expression of a true self once it has been located beneath convention
a comforting permission to relax the demands that society places upon the individual
questions.explanation: The passage states the self "is not discovered but made, and made continuously," while paragraph four insists we are "thrown into" conditions we did not select and must take them up. Option B captures both the making and the situatedness. A and C describe the "buried coin" excavation model the author explicitly rejects ("deny that any such buried coin exists"). D restates the popular "be yourself" slogan the author argues "inverts the actual difficulty"; he treats it as the misunderstanding to be corrected, not his thesis. Only B reflects the dual emphasis on continuous choosing and given conditions.

questions.stem: The author's overall tone toward the everyday phrase "be yourself" is best described as:
options: openly contemptuous of those who use it
critically corrective, treating it as a revealing distortion
nostalgic for an era when it carried weight
neutrally descriptive, neither endorsing nor faulting it
questions.explanation: The author says the slogan "inverts the actual difficulty" and "implies that the self is already there," then works to replace it with a better account—criticism aimed at correction, matching B. A ("contemptuous") is too harsh and personal; the author critiques the idea, not its users, with measured analysis rather than scorn. C imports nostalgia the text never expresses; the opening notes the phrase has been "worn" but longs for no past era. D fails because the author is plainly evaluative—calling the slogan a misunderstanding that "inverts" the truth—not neutral. B alone captures the corrective stance.

questions.stem: When the author writes that "the crowd is consoling precisely because it relieves us of authorship," the point of the remark is to:
options: praise communal life as a healthy antidote to excessive individualism
explain why conformity is appealing: it lets us evade responsibility for choosing
argue that social expectations are the source of a person's true preferences
suggest that authentic people must withdraw entirely from social groups
questions.explanation: The sentence appears amid the claim that what we evade is "the vertigo of this responsibility" and that doing as "one" does means "to disown the burden" of self-fashioning. So the remark explains conformity's appeal as an escape from responsibility—B. A reverses the point: the crowd is a refuge from a burden, not praised as healthy. C contradicts the text, which denies any pre-given preferences and treats borrowed opinions as evasion. D overreaches; the author never prescribes total withdrawal, only owning one's choices. B matches the explanatory function in context.

questions.stem: Which statement, if true, would most WEAKEN the author's rejection of the "buried coin" model of the self?
options: People frequently change their stated values after major life events
Individuals raised in different cultures express markedly different ambitions
Stable temperamental traits present in infancy reliably predict adult character despite varied upbringing
Many people report feeling liberated when they conform to group norms
questions.explanation: The author denies any "self anterior to its choices, waiting to be found." Evidence that fixed temperamental traits appear in infancy and persist into adulthood regardless of upbringing supports exactly the pre-given essence the author denies—so C most weakens the view. A fits the author's picture of a self continuously remade by choice, strengthening rather than weakening. B suggests environment shapes ambition, again consistent with a self that is made, not buried. D speaks to conformity's appeal, which the author already addresses and which does not bear on whether an essence precedes choice. Only C posits the durable, choice-independent self the author rejects.

questions.stem: The author concludes that "the surest sign of authenticity may be the refusal to believe one has attained it." This claim functions in the argument to:
options: concede that authenticity is finally impossible for anyone to achieve
show that the self, like a buried coin, can never be fully excavated
reinforce that authenticity is an unending task rather than a settled possession
introduce a new objection that undermines the preceding paragraphs
questions.explanation: The final paragraph explains that declaring oneself authentic "treated the self as a settled possession—and so betrayed the very freedom" at stake, tying back to the thesis that the self is "an ongoing achievement." The claim thus reinforces the task-not-possession view—C. A overstates: the author calls authenticity an ongoing achievement, not impossible, and people do choose authentically. B reuses the buried-coin image the author has rejected, so it cannot be the function here. D fails because the sentence completes and confirms the argument rather than undermining it. C correctly identifies its reinforcing, summative role.

questions.stem: A career counselor advises a client to take an aptitude test to "discover what you were always meant to do." Based on the passage, the author would most likely respond that this advice:
options: is sound, since uncovering native talents is the heart of authenticity
misconceives the self by assuming a destiny that precedes the client's choices
is acceptable only if the client also withdraws from social influence
should be rejected because all choices are determined by one's situation
questions.explanation: The phrase "meant to do" presumes a pre-given vocation awaiting discovery—precisely the buried-coin/essence model the author denies ("no self anterior to its choices"). So the author would say the advice misconceives the self—B. A endorses the excavation view the author rejects. C invents a withdrawal condition the passage never sets; the author insists we choose within, not apart from, our situation. D misreads the author as a determinist, but the text centers on "a freedom that must keep choosing" and holds us "answerable" for choices, the opposite of determinism. B alone applies the author's reasoning correctly.

title: Buildings That Argue
text:
We are accustomed to reading a building as a record of its time, a kind of frozen archive from which historians retrieve the tastes and technologies of a vanished public. But this archival metaphor, however convenient, understates what architecture actually does. A building does not merely store the values of those who raised it; it presses those values upon everyone who later passes through. A courthouse that compels visitors to ascend a long flight of steps before reaching a recessed, shadowed door is not neutrally recording a preference for grandeur. It is staging an argument about the relation between citizen and law, and it makes that argument every day, to people who never agreed to hear it.

This is why I am suspicious of the comfortable claim that architecture simply "reflects" a society. Reflection is passive; it implies a mirror that adds nothing. Yet a structure can advance a proposition its own builders never consciously held, and can keep advancing it long after they are gone. The medieval cathedral was, on one account, an expression of devotion. But once built, its soaring vault did not wait for the devout; it manufactured the very awe it was said to express, drawing even the indifferent into a posture of smallness before the infinite. The cause and effect, so neatly assumed, run in both directions.

It does not follow, however, that a building's argument is fixed or that we are helpless before it. Here I want to qualify the case I have been making. Meanings drift. The factory floor designed to discipline the worker into the rhythm of the machine becomes, two generations later, a loft prized for its light and its romance of honest labor — the same beams, the opposite sentiment. Use rewrites intention. If buildings argue, they argue in a language their occupants are forever revising, and a sufficiently determined public can talk back, can repurpose a grand staircase into a place to eat lunch, can render a monument quaint.

Still, I would resist the relativist conclusion that a building therefore means whatever we wish. The staircase can be ignored, but it cannot be wished into an elevator; its physical insistence sets the terms of any rebuttal. The argument from form is not a tyranny, but neither is it a suggestion one may simply decline. It is more like an inherited proposition that each generation must either ratify, contest, or laboriously dismantle — and dismantling, as anyone who has tried to demolish a well-made wall knows, is costly in a way that mere disagreement is not.

What follows for those who build? If a structure is an argument, then to design is to take a position, whether or not one means to. The architect who insists she is only solving a problem of circulation and light is, in the act of placing a wall, also deciding who may see whom, who waits and who is waited upon. Neutrality here is a polite fiction. The honest course is not to pretend the building says nothing but to ask what it says, and to whom, and at whose expense. A society that learns to read its buildings as arguments — rather than as mute reflections or inert archives — gains the standing to demand that those arguments be better ones. That, finally, is the civic value of treating stone and glass as a kind of speech: it makes the silent persuasion answerable.

questions.stem: The author's central thesis is best captured by which statement?
options: Buildings passively reflect the values of the societies that produce them.
Buildings actively make arguments that shape those who use them, though these arguments can be contested over time.
The meaning of a building is whatever its current occupants decide it should be.
Architecture is most valuable when architects strive for neutrality in their designs.
questions.explanation: The passage argues a building "presses those values" on users and "makes that argument every day," while later qualifying that "meanings drift" and a public "can talk back" — exactly option B. (A) is the "comfortable claim" the author is explicitly "suspicious of" (reflection is "passive"). (C) is the "relativist conclusion" the author says he would "resist," since form "cannot be wished into an elevator." (D) reverses the author, who calls neutrality "a polite fiction" and treats taking a position as unavoidable. Only B holds both the active-argument claim and its qualification together.

questions.stem: The author's overall attitude toward the idea that buildings "reflect" society can best be described as:
options: Skeptical, regarding the claim as too passive to capture what buildings do.
Hostile, dismissing the claim as entirely without merit.
Indifferent, treating the distinction as a matter of mere terminology.
Approving, since reflection accurately conveys a building's influence.
questions.explanation: The author writes "I am suspicious of the comfortable claim" and faults reflection as "passive" and as understating what architecture does — skeptical, matching A. (B) overstates: "suspicious" is critical but not a total dismissal, and he concedes the metaphor is "convenient." (C) is wrong because he treats the difference as substantive, not just verbal — the whole essay turns on it. (D) reverses his stance; he explicitly rejects reflection as inadequate. The calibration — criticism short of contempt — makes "skeptical" the best fit.

questions.stem: What is the primary function of the example of the factory floor that becomes a prized loft?
options: To prove that architects intend their buildings to change meaning over time.
To illustrate the author's qualification that a building's argument is not permanently fixed.
To demonstrate that buildings have no inherent argument at all.
To show that industrial buildings are superior to residential ones.
questions.explanation: The example immediately follows "Here I want to qualify the case I have been making" and "Meanings drift," showing intention can be rewritten by use — a qualification, so B. (A) inverts it: the new meaning arises despite the builders' intentions ("the opposite sentiment"), not by design. (C) is too strong; the author still insists form "sets the terms" and resists the view that buildings mean nothing.
