If there is a practical lesson, it is that the health of public opinion depends not on the impossible aspiration to frameless reporting, but on the live availability of genuine alternatives—and, no less, on the disposition of audiences to seek them out. The frame, after all, does its work only on those who never think to look past it.

questions.stem: Which of the following best captures the central thesis of the passage?
options: Media framing is an irresistible force that renders citizens passive recipients of elite interpretations.
Because framing is usually unintentional, its effects on public opinion are negligible and have been overstated.
Framing exerts a real but conditional influence, its power depending chiefly on whether competing frames are available.
The proliferation of media channels in the digital era has eliminated the danger that framing once posed.
questions.explanation: The author repeatedly insists the effect is "real but conditional," "powerful in the absence of contest and feeble in its presence," making (C) the thesis. (A) is the alarmism the author explicitly rejects ("resist...the alarmism that treats the audience as helpless"). (B) reverses the author, who argues unintended effects are not "illusory." (D) contradicts the claim that digital abundance "coincided with the consolidation" of dominant story-types and fragmented audiences into single-frame communities, leaving the danger intact.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the critics of the framing literature is best described as:
options: dismissive of an argument the author regards as wholly mistaken
partially appreciative but ultimately judging the criticism to overreach
wholly persuaded that framing effects have been exaggerated
indifferent to a dispute the author considers unresolvable
questions.explanation: The author calls the objection "salutary" (appreciative) yet says it "proves too much" and answers it point by point, so (B) fits exactly. (A) is too harsh: "salutary" signals partial agreement, not dismissal. (C) reverses the author, who defends framing's reality against the critics. (D) is contradicted by the author's active engagement and stated conclusions; the dispute is not treated as unresolvable but is adjudicated in favor of a conditional view of framing.

questions.stem: When the author writes that "we are defenseless against assumptions we do not notice we are making," the statement functions primarily to:
options: concede that the framing literature has no empirical support
explain why unintended framing can nonetheless be consequential
argue that audiences are permanently incapable of critical thought
introduce the claim that a homogeneous press is worse than a biased one
questions.explanation: The line appears in the rebuttal to the critics and supports the point that an effect's being unintended "does not render it illusory"; covert assumptions work "beneath the threshold of suspicion," so (B) names its function. (A) is wrong because the passage treats framing's influence as real, not unsupported. (C) overstates a permanent incapacity; later the author urges audiences to "seek out" alternatives, implying capacity. (D) misplaces the function—the homogeneous-press claim comes later and serves a different argument about competition among frames.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that the author would most likely regard a media environment with hundreds of outlets all drawing on the same official sources as:
options: reassuring, since the sheer number of outlets guarantees a diversity of frames
dangerous, because abundance of sources can mask a monopoly of frames
ideal, because commercial competition forces outlets to differentiate their narratives
irrelevant, because framing effects disappear once an issue becomes contested
questions.explanation: The author distinguishes "pluralism of sources" from "pluralism of frames" and warns that "a hundred outlets may reproduce an identical narrative" given shared routines and sources—precisely the danger of a single-frame monopoly. So (B). (A) is the error the author dismantles. (C) contradicts the claim that shared commercial incentives can homogenize rather than differentiate. (D) misapplies the contestation point: the scenario describes uniformity, i.e., no genuine rival frame, so effects would not disappear.

questions.stem: A study finds that a single newspaper's framing of a new tax strongly shaped readers' opinions in a town with no other news source, but had no measurable effect in a comparable town served by several ideologically varied outlets. This finding would most:
options: weaken the author's argument by showing framing effects are inconsistent and therefore unreal
strengthen the author's argument that framing is potent absent competing frames but attenuates when rivals exist
weaken the author's claim that a homogeneous press is more to be feared than a biased one
strengthen the critics' view that the public is a passive vessel for media interpretations
questions.explanation: The study matches the author's conditional thesis: framing was "most potent when an audience lacks...independent sources," and the effect "attenuates" once "a rival frame" appears. So it strengthens, (B). (A) misreads conditionality as unreality—the author already treats variation in effect as a matter of conditions, not illusion. (C) is wrong: the single-source town's strong effect supports, not undercuts, fearing homogeneity. (D) is backwards—the multi-outlet town's null effect shows audiences are not uniformly passive, cutting against the critics' strong claim.

questions.stem: Which hypothetical scenario is most analogous to the relationship the author describes between abundance of channels and consolidation of frames?
options: A library that doubles its number of books, each a distinct title on a different subject
A grocery store that stocks fifty brands of cereal, all manufactured by one company using the same recipe
A town that replaces its single radio station with a single television station
A school that hires more teachers, each trained in a different pedagogical tradition
questions.explanation: The author's paradox is apparent variety masking underlying uniformity—many outlets, one narrative, because of shared routines and sources. The cereal store (B) offers the illusion of choice (fifty brands) over genuine sameness (one company, one recipe), matching exactly. (A) and (D) depict real diversity beneath the abundance, the opposite of the author's point. (C) swaps one monopoly for another with no increase in apparent abundance, missing the central tension between surface variety and substantive sameness that drives the analogy.

title: The Honest Eye
text:
When the photograph first appeared, the most anxious among the painters declared that the new machine had killed their craft. The camera, they said, did nothing but copy. It demanded no hand, no temperament, no labor of imagination; it merely opened an eye of glass and let the world print itself. Their fear, though exaggerated, contained a stubborn truth. Of all the image-making arts, photography alone begins with a surrender. The painter may invent a tree that never grew; the photographer must stand before a tree that did. The medium is chained to the actual in a way that pigment and marble are not.

Yet to call this chain a limitation is to misunderstand the nature of seeing. We are tempted to imagine the eye as a passive window through which the world simply enters. But no one who has ever tried to make a photograph believes this. The world offers itself in inexhaustible abundance, and the photographer's first act is refusal. To frame is to exclude almost everything. The four edges of the picture are not boundaries the world possesses; they are decisions a person makes. In choosing where the rectangle falls, the photographer asserts that this, and not the endless else, deserves to be looked at. The recording, then, is never neutral. It is saturated with a prior act of attention.

Here the defenders of photography sometimes overreach. Eager to rescue the medium from the charge of mere mechanism, they insist that the photographer creates the image as fully as the painter creates a canvas. This claim proves too much. The painter answers to nothing but the inner vision; the photographer answers, always, to what was there. A portrait may flatter or wound, but it cannot conjure a face from nothing. The honesty of the medium is not a flaw to be apologized for but the very condition of its power. We trust a photograph in a way we never trust a painting, and that trust, however naive, is not entirely misplaced.

The resolution, I think, lies in distinguishing two things the word "seeing" conceals. There is seeing as registration, the optical fact that light has struck a surface and left its trace. And there is seeing as discernment, the human capacity to find significance in what registration delivers. The camera performs the first automatically; the photographer supplies the second or fails to. A thousand tourists may photograph the same monument, and their cameras will record it with equal fidelity. What separates the rare memorable image from the forgettable thousand is not the accuracy of the recording but the quality of the noticing. The machine guarantees the trace; it guarantees nothing about meaning.

This is why the old quarrel was poorly framed. To ask whether photography is an art of seeing or merely of recording is to assume the two are rivals. They are not. The recording is the ground on which the seeing stands. A photograph that recorded nothing would show us nothing to be seen; a photograph that merely recorded, without discernment, would be a fact and not a picture. The greatness of the medium is that it makes the photographer responsible for meaning precisely because it has relieved her of responsibility for appearance. Freed from the labor of imitation, she is left with the harder labor of judgment. The camera sees everything and understands nothing. That, not its mechanism, is the burden it hands to the one who holds it.

questions.stem: Which of the following best captures the author's central thesis about photography?
options: Photography is superior to painting because its honesty makes it more trustworthy.
Photography's mechanical recording and its human seeing are not opposed but interdependent.
Photography is merely a form of recording, and claims for its artistry are exaggerated.
Photography becomes art only when the photographer abandons fidelity to what was there.
questions.explanation: The final two paragraphs state the old quarrel was "poorly framed" because recording and seeing are not "rivals" but interdependent: "The recording is the ground on which the seeing stands," making B correct. A is wrong: the author calls our trust "naive" and never claims photography is superior to painting. C reverses the author, who argues photography demands discernment, not mere recording. D contradicts the author, who insists the photographer "answers, always, to what was there" and treats fidelity as "the very condition of its power," not something to abandon.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the photographer's "first act is refusal" because:
options: the photographer must reject the painters' claim that photography is mere copying
framing necessarily excludes almost all of the world's abundance
the photographer should refuse to flatter or distort the subject
honest recording requires rejecting any prior act of attention
questions.explanation: The second paragraph directly links refusal to framing: "To frame is to exclude almost everything," since the world offers "inexhaustible abundance" and the edges are "decisions a person makes." B states this. A imports the painters' debate from paragraph one, which is unrelated to the meaning of "refusal." C describes a portrait that may "flatter or wound" from paragraph three, not the act of refusal. D reverses the passage, which says recording is "saturated with a prior act of attention"—attention is embraced, not rejected.

questions.stem: The author distinguishes "seeing as registration" from "seeing as discernment" primarily in order to:
options: prove that the camera and the photographer perform identical functions
explain why two photographs recording a subject with equal fidelity can differ in value
argue that registration is more important to a photograph than discernment
demonstrate that discernment can occur without any registration at all
questions.explanation: The fourth paragraph deploys the distinction to explain the tourist example: cameras record "with equal fidelity," yet what separates the "memorable image from the forgettable thousand" is "the quality of the noticing," i.e., discernment—so B is correct. A is wrong: the passage insists the camera does registration "automatically" while the photographer supplies discernment, distinguishing them rather than equating them. C misstates the relationship; neither is ranked above the other—they are interdependent. D contradicts the final paragraph: "A photograph that recorded nothing would show us nothing to be seen," so discernment needs registration.

questions.stem: The author's treatment of "the defenders of photography" who say the photographer "creates the image as fully as the painter" suggests that the author:
options: fully endorses their view as the strongest defense of the medium
accepts their motive but rejects their conclusion as overstated
regards their argument as identical to the anxious painters' fear
believes photography has no claim to artistry whatsoever
questions.explanation: Paragraph three says these defenders are "eager to rescue the medium" (a sympathetic motive) but "sometimes overreach," and the claim "proves too much" because the photographer "answers, always, to what was there"—so B fits: accepts motive, rejects conclusion. A is wrong; the author explicitly faults the overreach. C is wrong: the defenders praise photography while the painters feared it killed their craft—opposite positions. D contradicts the whole passage, which argues photography demands the "harder labor of judgment" and is an art of meaning.

questions.stem: A documentary filmmaker argues that her work is creative because she decides which moments to film and which to leave out, even though her camera records events she cannot invent. This argument is most consistent with the passage's claim that:
options: recording and discernment are rivals that cannot coexist in one work
fidelity to the actual disqualifies a medium from being an art
selective attention, not invention, is the source of the medium's artistry
a creator's freedom from imitation removes all responsibility for meaning
questions.explanation: The filmmaker's reasoning mirrors the passage's view that the photographer is "chained to the actual" yet exercises art through choosing "where the rectangle falls"—selection and attention, not invention, supply artistry, making C correct. A reverses the author, who calls recording and seeing interdependent, not rivals. B contradicts the claim that fidelity is "the very condition of its power." D inverts the final paragraph: being "freed from the labor of imitation" leaves the creator with the "harder labor of judgment," i.e., more responsibility for meaning, not none.

questions.stem: Which finding would most WEAKEN the author's claim that "the quality of the noticing," rather than the accuracy of the recording, distinguishes memorable photographs?
options: A statistical study finds that the most accurate, technically precise recordings are reliably rated the most memorable images, regardless of framing or subject choice
Two photographers framing the same scene differently produce images viewers find equally memorable
Tourists photographing a monument produce images of varying technical sharpness
Skilled photographers report spending most of their effort deciding what to include in the frame
questions.explanation: The author claims memorability flows from discernment (the noticing), not from recording accuracy. A finding that the most technically accurate recordings are reliably the most memorable would relocate the source of memorability back to recording fidelity, directly undercutting the author—so A weakens it. B is consistent with the author, since different framing reflects differing discernment yet equal memorability says nothing against noticing. C merely notes technical variation without linking it to memorability. D directly supports the author by emphasizing selective attention, the opposite of weakening.

title: Weeping for Shadows
text:
There is something faintly absurd in our grief for people who never existed. We close a novel and find our throat tight over the death of a character we know to be a contrivance of ink, a figure the author could have spared with a stroke. The puzzle is not merely that we feel; it is that we feel knowingly. We do not, after all, scramble for the exits when the painted monster lunges across the screen. We are not deceived. Yet the tears are wet, the pulse genuine. How can a mind that disbelieves still tremble?

The simplest reply holds that we are momentarily fooled, that fiction works by a brief failure of the intellect. This explanation has the virtue of tidiness and the vice of falsehood. The reader who weeps does not believe a person has died; if she did, her response would be horror, not the strange, almost luxurious sorrow that literature affords. Indeed, the pleasure we take in tragic fiction is unthinkable if we mistook it for reportage. No one savors a stranger's actual funeral. The very safety of our knowledge that none of it is real is what licenses the depth of our feeling.

A subtler account proposes that our emotions are not directed at the characters at all but at thoughts—at the vivid imagining of suffering, regardless of whether anyone suffers. On this view, to be moved by a fiction is no stranger than to shudder at an imagined fall while standing safely on a balcony. The emotion attaches to the content of a mental image, and content needs no existing referent. This is closer to the truth, yet it threatens to prove too much. If mere imagining sufficed, we should weep equally at any sad scenario we could conjure unbidden, which we plainly do not. The novelist's labor would be superfluous.

What the deflationary theories overlook is craft. Emotion follows attention, and attention is something a fiction must earn. The skilled author does not present a sufficient sadness and trust us to supply the feeling; she engineers the sequence by which we come to care, withholding and disclosing, slowing time at the threshold of loss. Our tears are not evidence that we have forgotten the fictionality of what we read; they are evidence that we have been guided into a particular quality of attention that the world, in its disorder, rarely grants us. We mourn the character because the work has taught us, line by line, how to.

This suggests that the paradox dissolves once we abandon a cramped picture of what emotion is for. We tend to assume feelings are instruments of survival, alarms that should fire only at real threats and real losses. But emotions are also instruments of rehearsal and recognition. To weep for an invented orphan is to exercise a capacity for sorrow that would be monstrous to test on the living. Fiction is the laboratory in which we practice being the kind of creatures who can be moved—and, crucially, who can be moved by the right things.

Still, one should resist declaring the matter settled. The account I have sketched explains why we may feel, but not why we should welcome feelings procured by design, feelings that no fact in the world has earned. Perhaps there is a residual unease worth keeping. The reader who never wonders whether her manufactured grief is a kind of indulgence has perhaps not read deeply enough. The paradox of fiction may not be a problem to be solved so much as a tension to be inhabited—proof that our hearts are stranger, and more educable, than our theories of them allow.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the main reason the "we are momentarily fooled" explanation fails is that:
options: readers cannot distinguish painted monsters from real ones
a reader who genuinely believed in the death would respond with horror rather than sorrow
tragic fiction produces no pleasure of any kind
emotions are alarms designed to fire only at real threats
questions.explanation: The second paragraph states the weeping reader "does not believe a person has died; if she did, her response would be horror, not the strange, almost luxurious sorrow that literature affords." That mismatch is the stated reason the deception theory is false. Option A reverses the passage, which insists "We are not deceived." Option C is too extreme; the passage affirms "the pleasure we take in tragic fiction" exists. Option D describes the "cramped picture" of emotion the author later critiques, not the reason the deception theory fails. Only B names the actual argument: the felt emotion is the wrong kind for genuine belief.

questions.stem: The passage indicates that the author regards the "thoughts/imagining" account as:
options: entirely mistaken and no better than the deception theory
correct and sufficient as a complete solution
an improvement that nonetheless overreaches
irrelevant to the paradox of fiction
questions.explanation: The author calls this account "subtler" and "closer to the truth," but warns it "threatens to prove too much": if mere imagining sufficed, we'd weep at any conjured scenario, which we don't. That is partial endorsement plus a stated limitation, matching C. Option A is too harsh; the author explicitly ranks it above the deception view. Option B ignores the "too much" objection and the later emphasis on craft. Option D contradicts the text, which treats the account as a direct, relevant contender. The credited answer captures both praise and qualification.

questions.stem: The author introduces the role of "craft" primarily in order to:
options: prove that readers are in fact deceived by skilled authors
explain why imagining alone does not reliably produce strong emotion
argue that emotions serve no survival function
show that all sad scenarios move us equally
questions.explanation: Craft is introduced right after the objection that imagining proves "too much"—we do not weep at every sad thing we conjure. The author says "Emotion follows attention, and attention is something a fiction must earn," so craft guides us into a particular quality of attention. Thus it explains the gap the imagining account leaves, supporting B. Option A reverses the text's repeated insistence that we are not deceived. Option C overstates; the author later grants emotions a survival role before adding others. Option D contradicts the very claim craft is meant to answer—that conjured scenes do not move us equally.

questions.stem: Which statement best captures the function of the passage's final paragraph?
options: It retracts the craft-based account developed earlier
It concedes a question the author's account leaves unanswered
It restates the deception theory as the correct view
It proves that manufactured grief is morally wrong
questions.explanation: The final paragraph says the account "explains why we may feel, but not why we should welcome" designed feelings, and recommends "a residual unease worth keeping." This concedes an open normative question rather than overturning the prior argument, matching B. Option A is wrong: the author does not abandon the craft account, only notes its limit. Option C contradicts the passage, which dismissed deception early. Option D overreaches; the author merely raises whether grief might be "a kind of indulgence" and calls the matter a "tension to be inhabited," not a proven wrong. B alone reflects the deliberate non-closure.

questions.stem: A study finds that readers report markedly stronger sorrow for a fictional death after reading a long, carefully paced lead-up than after reading a one-sentence summary of the same death. This finding most directly supports the passage's claim that:
options: readers are deceived into believing the character is real
emotion follows attention that a fiction must earn through craft
tragic fiction gives no more pleasure than reportage
any imagined sad scenario moves us as much as any other
questions.explanation: The passage argues the author "engineers the sequence by which we come to care, withholding and disclosing, slowing time," so feeling tracks earned attention, not raw content. A paced lead-up producing more sorrow than a bare summary directly instantiates B. Option A is contradicted by the passage's insistence that we are not deceived, and the study says nothing about belief. Option C is irrelevant and contradicts the text's claim that fiction's pleasure depends on knowing it is unreal. Option D is the very position the passage denies; identical content with different pacing yielding different sorrow refutes, not supports, it.

questions.stem: Suppose a critic argued that grief for fictional characters is simply a malfunction—an emotional alarm misfiring at a non-threat. The author would most likely respond that this critic:
options: is correct, since feelings should fire only at real losses
mistakes one function of emotion for its only function
has proven that fiction should be avoided
agrees with the craft-based account of attention
questions.explanation: The author grants that emotions are partly "instruments of survival, alarms that should fire only at real threats," but insists they are "also instruments of rehearsal and recognition," a laboratory for "practice." The critic's view treats the survival function as the whole story, which the author explicitly rejects, so B fits. Option A endorses the "cramped picture" the author argues against. Option C overstates; the author finds value in fiction as practice, not grounds for avoidance. Option D is wrong: the alarm-malfunction view ignores craft and attention entirely, so the author would not say the critic agrees with that account.

title: The Labor of the Difficult
text:
There is a persistent suspicion, voiced as often by sophisticated critics as by the casual public, that difficulty in art is a kind of fraud. The obscure poem, the dissonant symphony, the canvas that resists any nameable subject—these, the suspicion runs, are screens behind which the artist hides a poverty of feeling, or worse, the absence of anything to say. On this view, clarity is the natural condition of honest expression, and whatever cannot be readily grasped has failed in the one task art is for: to communicate. I want to resist this conclusion, though not by denying that fraudulent obscurity exists. It plainly does. The question is whether difficulty as such is the flaw, or whether the flaw lies elsewhere and difficulty is merely its most visible symptom.

Consider what we ask of a work when we demand that it be easy. We ask that it meet us where we already are—that it confirm the categories, rhythms, and expectations we bring to it. An art that only does this is pleasant, and pleasantness is not nothing. But it is also, in a precise sense, conservative: it leaves the viewer exactly as it found her. The difficult work, by contrast, refuses the terms of immediate recognition. It withholds the satisfaction of being understood at once, and in that withholding it issues a demand—that we revise the very faculties by which we attend to it. The reward, when there is one, is not merely the eventual decoding of a hidden message. It is the enlargement of perception itself.

This is the strongest case for difficulty, and I find it genuinely persuasive. Yet it carries a danger I cannot ignore. If difficulty enlarges us, it becomes tempting to treat the experience of struggle as proof of value—to assume that whatever taxes us must therefore be improving us. This inference is false. There is labor that leads nowhere, effort spent on works that conceal not a deeper order but simple incoherence. The struggle feels identical in both cases; from the inside, the reader cannot always tell genuine obscurity from its counterfeit. Difficulty, then, is no guarantee of depth. At most it is a precondition for a certain kind of depth, one that easy art cannot reach.

How might we tell the two apart? Not, I think, by any test applied at the moment of encounter, for the counterfeit is designed precisely to survive such tests. The distinction reveals itself only over time, in whether the work continues to yield. The genuinely difficult work rewards return: each re-encounter discloses relations invisible before, and the obscurity that once seemed an obstacle comes to look like the only adequate form for what is being said. The fraudulent work, by contrast, exhausts itself. Once its trick is named, nothing remains; rereading discovers no new country, only the same flat puzzle.

I am aware that this criterion is retrospective and therefore unsatisfying to anyone who wants a verdict now. But perhaps that impatience is itself the thing to be questioned. The demand that art prove its worth on first contact may be less a standard of honesty than a refusal of the patience art sometimes requires. Difficulty, on this account, is not a virtue in itself—nothing is gained by mere inaccessibility—but it can be the necessary cost of an ambition that easy art, by its nature, declines to undertake. To honor that ambition is not to excuse every obscurity. It is only to insist that we not mistake our discomfort for a diagnosis.

questions.stem: The author's primary purpose in the passage is to:
options: demonstrate that difficult art is consistently superior to art that is immediately accessible.
argue that while difficulty can serve a valuable artistic purpose, it is not by itself proof of merit.
expose the obscure poem and the dissonant symphony as characteristic instances of artistic fraud.
establish a reliable test for distinguishing genuine art from counterfeit at the moment of first encounter.
questions.explanation: The author repeatedly qualifies the defense of difficulty, stating difficulty is 'no guarantee of depth' and 'not a virtue in itself,' yet 'can be the necessary cost' of ambition—matching option B. Option A overstates: the author concedes pleasant easy art 'is not nothing.' Option C reverses the author, who cites those works as examples critics wrongly suspect, then resists that suspicion. Option D contradicts the passage explicitly: the distinguishing criterion 'is retrospective,' revealing itself 'only over time,' never at first encounter. B alone captures the balanced, qualified stance the author builds across the essay.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the author characterizes art that is easy to grasp as "conservative" because such art:
options: deliberately conceals a poverty of feeling behind a screen of accessibility.
leaves the viewer's existing faculties and expectations unchanged.
is favored by sophisticated critics over the casual public.
exhausts itself once its central trick has been named.
questions.explanation: The passage defines the term directly: easy art 'leaves the viewer exactly as it found her,' confirming 'the categories, rhythms, and expectations we bring to it'—option B. Option A describes the author's account of fraudulent OBSCURITY, not easy art. Option C misreads: the suspicion of difficulty is shared by 'sophisticated critics' and 'the casual public' alike, and this isn't tied to 'conservative.' Option D describes the FRAUDULENT difficult work that 'exhausts itself,' not easy art. Only B reflects the author's explicit reason: easy art is conservative in 'a precise sense' because it produces no change in the perceiver.

questions.stem: The author argues that one cannot reliably distinguish genuine from fraudulent difficulty at the moment of encounter. Which claim in the passage most directly supports this position?
options: Pleasantness 'is not nothing,' so easy art retains a legitimate value.
The reward of difficult art is 'the enlargement of perception itself.'
From the inside, the reader 'cannot always tell genuine obscurity from its counterfeit,' since the struggle feels identical.
Fraudulent obscurity 'plainly' exists and the author does not deny it.
questions.explanation: The support for the no-immediate-test claim is that the experience of struggle is the same in both cases: the reader 'cannot always tell genuine obscurity from its counterfeit'—option C. Option A concerns the value of easy art, irrelevant to the genuine/fraudulent distinction. Option B states the reward of genuine difficulty but says nothing about why the two are hard to tell apart in the moment. Option D establishes that fraud exists but not that it is indistinguishable at encounter. Only C gives the reason—identical phenomenology of struggle—that grounds the author's claim that no contemporaneous test works.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that the author would most likely regard a reader who abandons a difficult work after one frustrating attempt as:
options: correctly applying the only valid test of artistic worth.
possibly mistaking impatience for a sound judgment of the work's value.
rightly protecting herself from the fraud that obscurity usually conceals.
demonstrating the enlarged perception that difficult art produces.
questions.explanation: The author questions 'the demand that art prove its worth on first contact,' calling it possibly 'a refusal of the patience art sometimes requires' and warning against mistaking 'our discomfort for a diagnosis'—so a one-attempt abandonment risks confusing impatience with judgment (B). Option A contradicts the author, for whom the valid criterion is retrospective return, not first-attempt frustration. Option C assumes obscurity 'usually' conceals fraud, which the author never claims—he treats genuine and fraudulent difficulty as both real. Option D reverses the point: abandoning the work forecloses the return that yields enlarged perception. B follows directly from the closing caution.

questions.stem: A novel is initially praised as profound for its fragmented, hard-to-follow structure, but critics who reread it years later find that it offers nothing they had not already seen on first reading. How does this case relate to the author's argument?
options: It refutes the author, by showing that difficulty and depth are unrelated.
It illustrates the author's notion of fraudulent difficulty, which 'exhausts itself' upon return.
It supports the author's claim that difficulty is a virtue in itself.
It shows that the retrospective criterion the author proposes cannot actually be applied.
questions.explanation: The author's mark of fraudulent difficulty is that it 'exhausts itself'—'once its trick is named, nothing remains,' and 'rereading discovers no new country.' The novel that yields nothing on return is exactly this case (B). Option A overgeneralizes: the case shows THIS work lacked depth, not that difficulty and depth are 'unrelated'—the author holds difficulty can be a precondition for depth. Option C contradicts the author, who denies difficulty is 'a virtue in itself.' Option D is wrong: the case shows the retrospective test being successfully applied, not failing. B fits the author's framework precisely.

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most WEAKEN the author's claim that genuine and fraudulent difficulty can be distinguished only over time and not at first encounter?
options: Some works initially judged profound are later found to be empty puzzles.
A trained perceptual test administered on first contact reliably predicts which difficult works will continue to reward rereading.
Most viewers prefer pleasant art that confirms their existing expectations.
Artists sometimes produce obscurity to conceal that they have nothing to say.
questions.explanation: The author insists the distinction reveals itself 'only over time' and that no 'test applied at the moment of encounter' can work because the counterfeit survives such tests. A reliable first-contact test predicting which works reward rereading directly contradicts this—option B. Option A is consistent with the author, illustrating fraud, not undermining the timing claim. Option C concerns audience preference, not whether the distinction can be made early. Option D restates a premise the author already accepts (fraud exists). Only B targets the specific load-bearing claim—that no contemporaneous test suffices—and so most weakens it.

title: The Earnest Mask
text:
There is a persistent suspicion that irony is the enemy of conviction. The ironist, on this view, is a person who has retreated from belief into a defensive crouch, saying one thing while meaning another precisely so that nothing can be pinned on him. He keeps his commitments at arm's length, ready to disown any of them should they prove embarrassing. Against the earnest reformer who plants his flag and stands by it, the ironist appears slippery, even cowardly—a connoisseur of attitudes who has mistaken detachment for depth.

This portrait is not wholly false, and it would be foolish to deny that irony can serve as evasion. There is a familiar species of cleverness that uses the ironic register as a kind of insurance policy: one ventures a provocative claim, and when challenged, retreats to the safety of "I wasn't being serious." Such irony is indeed a failure of nerve dressed up as sophistication. But to treat this degraded form as the whole of irony is to mistake the counterfeit for the coin.

Consider the moralist who confronts a society convinced of its own decency while it tolerates manifest cruelty. To denounce that cruelty directly is to be dismissed as a scold, easily filed under the heading of predictable outrage and thereby neutralized. The ironist takes a different route. By appearing to praise the very arrangements he despises—by adopting, with exquisite care, the complacent voice of the comfortable—he forces his audience to hear their own assumptions spoken aloud. The praise is so excessive, so attentive to the gap between what is said and what is true, that it becomes unbearable. Here irony is not a hedge but a scalpel. It commits the speaker more deeply than direct denunciation could, because it stakes everything on the audience's capacity to feel the wrongness for themselves.

What distinguishes this serious irony from its cowardly cousin is the direction of its risk. The evasive ironist protects himself; the serious ironist exposes himself. He gambles that his listeners will complete the meaning he has only gestured toward, and if they fail to, his utterance collapses into apparent endorsement of the very thing he loathes. He has surrendered the comfort of being correctly understood. This is not detachment but a peculiar form of intimacy: he trusts his audience to do the moral work, refusing to do it for them in the patronizing manner of the lecturer.

One might object that such irony is elitist, intelligible only to those already equipped to decode it, and therefore useless as moral persuasion. The objection has force, but it misreads the aim. The serious ironist is not chiefly trying to convert the oblivious; he is trying to dignify his hearers by treating them as capable of judgment. The earnest moralist, by contrast, often flatters himself that he alone sees clearly, and his very directness can carry a hidden contempt for an audience presumed too dull to arrive at the truth unassisted.

It does not follow that irony is always superior to earnestness, nor that the sincere reformer is secretly the more arrogant figure. There are moments—the witnessing of atrocity, the consoling of the grieving—when irony would be obscene, when only plain speech will do. The claim is narrower and stranger: that irony, in its serious mode, can be a discipline of moral attention rather than a flight from it, and that we mistake its nature when we assume the smile must always conceal an empty hand.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the "degraded form" of irony functions primarily as:
options: a scalpel that exposes hidden cruelty
a means of dignifying the audience's judgment
an insurance policy that lets the speaker disown his claims
a discipline of sustained moral attention
questions.explanation: The passage describes the degraded form as "a kind of insurance policy: one ventures a provocative claim, and when challenged, retreats to the safety of 'I wasn't being serious,'" making option 3 correct. Option 1 describes serious irony ("a scalpel"), the opposite of the degraded form. Option 2 attributes to the counterfeit a function the passage reserves for serious irony, which "dignif[ies] his hearers." Option 4 reverses the author's terms: the degraded form is precisely "a flight from" moral attention, not a discipline of it. Each distractor borrows language the author applies to the serious mode and misassigns it to the cowardly mode.

questions.stem: The passage indicates that what most clearly separates serious irony from its evasive counterpart is:
options: the cleverness of the language employed
the direction of the risk the speaker takes on
whether the audience is educated enough to decode it
the sincerity of the speaker's underlying beliefs
questions.explanation: The author states this directly: "What distinguishes this serious irony from its cowardly cousin is the direction of its risk. The evasive ironist protects himself; the serious ironist exposes himself." Option 2 is credited. Option 1 fails because cleverness characterizes both modes and is never offered as the distinguishing feature. Option 3 concerns the elitism objection, which the author addresses separately and treats as a misreading of the aim, not as the defining difference. Option 4 is tempting but unsupported as the distinguishing criterion; the passage locates the difference in risk and exposure, not in measuring underlying sincerity.

questions.stem: The author's claim that serious irony involves "a peculiar form of intimacy" rests on the idea that the ironist:
options: openly shares his deepest convictions with the audience
trusts the audience to supply the meaning he leaves implicit
conceals his identity to avoid social consequences
persuades the oblivious by repeating his point plainly
questions.explanation: The passage explains the intimacy thus: "he trusts his audience to do the moral work, refusing to do it for them," and he "gambles that his listeners will complete the meaning he has only gestured toward." Option 2 captures this. Option 1 contradicts the text: the ironist gestures rather than states openly, and even "surrender[s] the comfort of being correctly understood." Option 3 describes the evasive ironist's self-protection, the opposite of intimacy. Option 4 describes the earnest lecturer's directness, which the author explicitly distinguishes from the ironist's method of "refusing to do it for them in the patronizing manner of the lecturer."

questions.stem: The passage suggests that the author would most likely regard a directly stated moral denunciation as risky because it can:
options: be too sophisticated for ordinary audiences to follow
be neutralized by being filed as predictable outrage
commit the speaker more deeply than he intends
expose the speaker to disowning his own claims
questions.explanation: The author writes that to denounce cruelty directly "is to be dismissed as a scold, easily filed under the heading of predictable outrage and thereby neutralized," supporting option 2. Option 1 reverses the elitism concern, which the author raises about irony, not direct denunciation. Option 3 misapplies a feature the author assigns to serious irony, which "commits the speaker more deeply than direct denunciation could." Option 4 describes the evasive ironist's escape hatch, not a hazard of plain denunciation; the direct speaker's problem is being dismissed, not the temptation to disown what he said.

questions.stem: A critic argues that any communication depending on the audience to infer unstated meaning is inherently unreliable and should be avoided in moral discourse. The author would most likely respond that this critic:
options: correctly identifies why irony must be abandoned entirely
overlooks that such dependence can dignify the audience as capable of judgment
agrees with the author's view that plain speech is always preferable
fails to see that serious irony carries no risk of misunderstanding
questions.explanation: The author treats the audience's interpretive labor not as a defect but as respect: the ironist "dignif[ies] his hearers by treating them as capable of judgment." Thus the author would say the critic overlooks this, making option 2 correct. Option 1 overstates; the author defends serious irony rather than abandoning it. Option 3 misstates the author's position—he holds plain speech is required only in specific moments (atrocity, grief), not always. Option 4 contradicts the text, which insists serious irony explicitly risks collapsing "into apparent endorsement" if misunderstood; the author concedes the risk rather than denying it.

questions.stem: Which scenario best exemplifies the "serious irony" the author defends, as opposed to its degraded form?
options: A comedian mocks a politician, then says he was only joking when criticized
An essayist solemnly commends a city's charity while detailing the misery it ignores
A reformer plainly lists a regime's abuses to rally public anger
A speaker hints at a controversial view, ready to deny he ever held it
questions.explanation: Serious irony, per the author, adopts "the complacent voice of the comfortable" with praise so excessive that the audience feels the wrongness themselves. Option 2 matches: solemn commendation set against the misery it ignores forces hearers to confront the gap. Options 1 and 4 are textbook degraded irony—the "insurance policy" of retreating to "I wasn't being serious" or readiness to deny. Option 3 is earnest, direct denunciation, which the author distinguishes from irony altogether and notes risks being dismissed as a scold. Only option 2 enacts the committed, self-exposing strategy the author calls serious.

title: Guarding the Gate
text:
To ask who decides what counts as a great work is to assume that someone, in fact, decides. The image is seductive: a committee of arbiters, a college of taste, conferring permanence upon a chosen few. But the canon has never operated like a legislature. No body ever convened to vote a poem into immortality. What we call the canon is better understood as a sediment—the slow accumulation of countless small acts of preservation, citation, and teaching, each performed by people who imagined they were merely passing along what they had received.

This is why the familiar quarrel between two camps so often misfires. On one side stand the defenders of intrinsic merit, who insist that great works are great because of qualities they possess: formal mastery, depth of insight, the capacity to reward rereading. On the other stand the skeptics, who reply that "merit" is a mask for power—that works survive because they flatter the prejudices of those who control schools, presses, and prizes. Each side accuses the other of naivety, and each is partly right. The defenders are correct that not every text rewards attention equally; the skeptics are correct that attention itself is never distributed by accident.

The error lies in treating merit and power as rivals, as though a work owed its standing to one or the other. Consider instead that they are entangled. A work cannot reveal its depths to readers who never encounter it, and encounter is precisely what institutions ration. Yet institutions do not preserve at random; over generations, the texts that survive are disproportionately those that *can* sustain renewed attention, because a work that exhausts itself on first reading gives later custodians little reason to keep teaching it. Power opens the door, but something in the work must persuade each new generation to keep the door open.

Here a complication enters that neither camp anticipates. The very act of canonization alters the object it honors. A poem read because it is required is not read as the poem its first audience encountered; centuries of commentary, imitation, and reverence accrete around it until we can no longer easily distinguish the work's qualities from the qualities our attention has lent it. The canon, in other words, is partly self-fulfilling. We find depth in the works we have decided to keep returning to, and our returning manufactures some of the very depth we then cite as the reason for our return.

This should chasten the confidence of both parties without licensing despair. It does not follow that all judgments are arbitrary. That a building's foundations were laid by interested hands does not mean the building will stand; some structures collapse, and some works, once elevated, quietly lose their hold on readers and drift out of the conversation despite every institutional effort to retain them. The canon is revisable, and its revisions are not merely changes of fashion—they often register genuine discoveries about what a work can and cannot do.

What emerges, then, is neither a monument nor a conspiracy but a wager that each generation renews or declines to renew. To enter the canon is to be bet upon; to remain is to keep winning bets that no one remembers placing. The question is not whether greatness is "real" or "constructed," for it is plainly both. The more exacting question is whether we can tell, from inside a tradition we did not choose, which of our admirations we would still hold if the door had opened elsewhere.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the canon is best understood as:
options: the product of a deliberative body that confers permanence on selected works
an accumulation of small, dispersed acts of preservation, citation, and teaching
a fixed monument that resists all revision over time
a list assembled chiefly to flatter the prejudices of the powerful
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly states the canon "is better understood as a sediment—the slow accumulation of countless small acts of preservation, citation, and teaching," making B correct. A inverts the author's claim; the opening calls the committee image "seductive" but denies that any body "ever convened to vote a poem into immortality." C contradicts the later assertion that "the canon is revisable." D states only the skeptics' view, which the author treats as "partly right" but not as a full account, since the author rejects framing the canon as merely an instrument of power.

questions.stem: The passage indicates that the author regards the two opposing camps (defenders of intrinsic merit and skeptics) as:
options: each wholly mistaken in its core assumption
each partly correct but wrong to treat merit and power as rivals
fundamentally identical in their conclusions
irreconcilable, with the skeptics ultimately vindicated
questions.explanation: The author writes "each is partly right," then locates "the error" in "treating merit and power as rivals," supporting B. A is too extreme: the author credits each side with a genuine insight, not total error. C is false; the camps reach opposite conclusions, not identical ones. D misreads the stance, since the author does not vindicate the skeptics but argues both sides miss the entanglement of merit and power, and even insists judgments are not "arbitrary," pushing against the skeptics' implied relativism.

questions.stem: The author claims the canon is "partly self-fulfilling" primarily because:
options: institutions deliberately conspire to inflate the reputations of favored authors
sustained attention to a work generates some of the depth later cited to justify that attention
first audiences always misjudge the works they encounter
works of genuine merit can never lose their canonical status
questions.explanation: The passage explains that "our returning manufactures some of the very depth we then cite as the reason for our return," matching B. A imports a "conspiracy" the author explicitly disavows in the conclusion ("neither a monument nor a conspiracy"). C overstates: the author says canonized works are not read as first audiences read them, not that those audiences "misjudged." D contradicts the passage, which says some elevated works "quietly lose their hold" and "drift out of the conversation," so canonical status is not permanent.

questions.stem: The author's claim that "some structures collapse" most directly serves to:
options: prove that intrinsic merit is the sole determinant of canonical survival
show that institutional power is irrelevant to which works endure
resist the inference that interested origins make all canonical judgments arbitrary
demonstrate that the canon never undergoes meaningful revision
questions.explanation: The building analogy follows the line "This should chasten the confidence of both parties without licensing despair" and the claim that it does "not follow that all judgments are arbitrary," so the collapse image rebuts the leap from interested origins to arbitrariness, which is C. A overstates: the author insists merit and power are "entangled," not that merit alone decides. B contradicts the claim that "power opens the door." D reverses the passage, which affirms the canon "is revisable" and that revisions "register genuine discoveries," the opposite of denying revision.

questions.stem: Suppose a long-celebrated novel, after decades of reassessment, is widely judged shallow and disappears from syllabi despite publishers' efforts to keep it in print. This outcome would most strengthen which of the author's claims?
options: that encounter with a work is rationed entirely by institutions
that canonical revisions can register genuine discoveries about a work rather than mere fashion
that merit is wholly a mask for institutional power
that no work can ever lose its place once admitted
questions.explanation: The scenario shows a work losing standing on grounds of reassessed quality despite institutional effort, mirroring the author's claim that revisions "often register genuine discoveries about what a work can and cannot do," which is B. A is unsupported and partly contradicted, since here institutions tried and failed to retain the work, showing encounter is not the whole story. C is the skeptics' extreme view the author rejects; the example actually undercuts pure power-explanations. D is directly contradicted by the example, in which an admitted work loses its place.

questions.stem: A critic argues: "Because every reader inherits a tradition they did not choose, no one can ever judge a work's greatness independently of that inheritance." How would the author most likely respond?
options: Agree fully, since the passage denies that any independent judgment is possible
Accept that inheritance shapes judgment, but deny this makes all judgments equally valid or unrevisable
Reject the premise, insisting readers can step entirely outside their tradition
Concede that greatness is therefore purely a matter of fashion
questions.explanation: The author acknowledges we judge "from inside a tradition we did not choose" yet insists it does "not follow that all judgments are arbitrary" and that the canon is genuinely "revisable," so the author accepts the critic's premise about inheritance while resisting the relativist conclusion, matching B. A overstates; the author allows revisable, non-arbitrary judgment. C contradicts the closing acknowledgment that we cannot simply choose our tradition. D embraces the "matter of fashion" view the author explicitly denies when calling revisions more than "merely changes of fashion."

title: The Eloquence of the Unsaid
text:
We habitually treat literature as an art of saying, prizing the writer who finds words for what others leave inchoate. Yet some of the most consequential effects in narrative depend not on what is articulated but on what is withheld. A character's refusal to answer, a sentence broken off, the conspicuous gap where an explanation should sit—these absences are not failures of expression. They are expression of a particular and demanding kind, and a theory of literary meaning that cannot account for them is incomplete.

The skeptic will object that silence carries no meaning in itself; meaning is supplied entirely by the reader, who projects significance onto a blank. There is something to this. An unmarked pause on the page is, considered alone, merely typographic. But the objection proves too much, for it would dissolve all literary meaning into reader projection. The word "loss" on a page is likewise inert ink until a reader animates it. What matters is not whether the reader contributes but whether the text governs that contribution. A well-made silence does govern it: the surrounding language has so prepared the moment that only certain inferences are licensed. When a grieving figure is asked how she feels and the narrative simply moves on, the omission is not an empty space awaiting any meaning the reader pleases. It is a shaped space, and its shape constrains.

Here, though, we must qualify the enthusiasm. Not every absence is eloquent. Much that goes unsaid in a text is unsaid because it is irrelevant, and to read every gap as pregnant is to mistake one's own ingenuity for the author's design. The interpreter who finds profundity in a mere ellipsis risks the very projection the skeptic warned of. The distinction lies in preparation. An eloquent silence is earned: the text builds an expectation of speech and then denies it, so that the denial itself becomes legible. An accidental gap builds no such expectation and denies nothing.

This is why the unsaid functions differently from the merely unwritten. Everything a text does not say is unwritten; only a fraction is genuinely unsaid in the charged sense, where the omission is felt as omission. The felt quality is the criterion. We sense that something has been declined, not merely left out, and that sense is itself produced by the writing. Paradoxically, then, the most powerful silences are the most authored. They are not gaps in the writer's control but its furthest extension—the point at which an author trusts language to do its work by stepping aside.

One might press further and ask whether such silences are a higher achievement than statement, as some critics imply when they exalt restraint over disclosure. I think this overreaches. Restraint is not a virtue independent of what it restrains. A withheld answer is powerful only against the pressure of a question that demanded one; remove the pressure and the withholding collapses into mere vagueness. Silence, in short, is parasitic on speech. It cannot be the foundation of meaning, only its sharpest instrument. The writer who would say nothing must first have built a world in which something was urgently expected. The unsaid earns its force from the said, and to forget this is to romanticize absence into a mystique it does not deserve.

questions.stem: Which of the following best captures the author's central claim about literary silence?
options: Silence is the highest form of literary expression, surpassing direct statement.
Silence can carry meaning when the text has prepared the reader to register an absence as deliberate.
Silence has no meaning of its own and is entirely a product of reader projection.
Silence and statement are equally effective regardless of how a text is constructed.
questions.explanation: The author argues throughout that an 'eloquent silence is earned'—the surrounding language must so prepare the moment that the omission is 'felt as omission,' making option B correct. Option A reverses the author's explicit rejection of critics who 'exalt restraint over disclosure'; the author calls this 'overreach.' Option C states the skeptic's position, which the author entertains but refutes ('the objection proves too much'). Option D ignores the central insistence that meaning depends on preparation—the text must 'govern' the reader's contribution—so silences are not effective 'regardless' of construction.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the difference between the 'unwritten' and the genuinely 'unsaid' is that the unsaid is:
options: composed of words the author originally drafted but later deleted.
any content a text happens not to include.
felt by the reader as a deliberate omission rather than a mere absence.
always more profound than anything the text states directly.
questions.explanation: The passage states 'only a fraction is genuinely unsaid in the charged sense, where the omission is felt as omission,' and names 'the felt quality' as 'the criterion,' making C correct. Option B describes the merely 'unwritten' ('Everything a text does not say is unwritten')—precisely the category contrasted with the unsaid. Option A invents a drafting history the passage never mentions; the distinction is about reader experience, not composition process. Option D imports the 'higher achievement' claim the author explicitly rejects as overreaching, conflating the unsaid with superiority rather than with felt deliberateness.

questions.stem: The author responds to the skeptic's objection that silence carries no inherent meaning primarily by arguing that:
options: the objection, if accepted, would also eliminate the meaning of ordinary words.
readers never actually contribute anything to a text's meaning.
silence is in fact more meaningful than the skeptic could imagine.
the skeptic has confused typography with significance.
questions.explanation: The author concedes 'there is something to this' but says 'the objection proves too much, for it would dissolve all literary meaning into reader projection,' noting the word 'loss' is also 'inert ink until a reader animates it'—option A. Option B reverses the author, who grants the reader does contribute and asks only 'whether the text governs that contribution.' Option C overstates a claim the author never makes here and ignores the structure of the rebuttal. Option D misattributes; the author uses the typographic point to grant the skeptic's premise, not to accuse the skeptic of confusion.

questions.stem: The author's warning that an interpreter may 'mistake one's own ingenuity for the author's design' functions in the argument to:
options: abandon the claim that silence can be meaningful.
concede partial validity to the skeptic's worry about projection while preserving the author's main thesis.
prove that all gaps in a text are equally significant.
argue that authorial intention is irrelevant to interpretation.
questions.explanation: The qualification admits 'much that goes unsaid... is unsaid because it is irrelevant' and that over-reading 'risks the very projection the skeptic warned of'—a concession—yet the author immediately preserves the thesis via 'preparation,' distinguishing earned from accidental silence (option B). Option A overstates: the author qualifies but does not abandon the claim. Option C reverses the point, which is that gaps are NOT equally significant. Option D contradicts the passage, which centers 'the author's design' and calls powerful silences 'the most authored,' making intention central rather than irrelevant.

questions.stem: A novelist ends a chapter mid-sentence as a character begins to confess a crime, with no further reference to the confession anywhere in the book. Based on the passage, the author would most likely say this silence is meaningful only if:
options: the reader is creative enough to imagine several possible confessions.
the surrounding narrative has built an expectation of disclosure that the break then denies.
the omission is the most profound moment in the entire novel.
the novelist deleted the original confession during revision.
questions.explanation: The author insists an eloquent silence is 'earned'—'the text builds an expectation of speech and then denies it, so that the denial itself becomes legible'—matching B. Option A reflects the skeptic's projection view the author rejects; mere reader creativity is not the criterion, textual governance is. Option C imports the 'higher achievement'/profundity claim the author explicitly dismisses as overreach and is not a condition for meaning. Option D concerns composition history, which the passage treats as irrelevant; the test is the reader's felt sense of deliberate denial, produced by the text, not by drafting decisions.

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most WEAKEN the author's claim that 'silence is parasitic on speech'?
options: A reader reports feeling moved by a long pause in a poem.
Texts that establish strong expectations of speech produce the most powerful silences.
Some readers experience profound meaning in works composed entirely of blank pages, with no expectation-setting language at all.
A withheld answer loses its force when the question prompting it is removed.
questions.explanation: The author claims silence 'cannot be the foundation of meaning, only its sharpest instrument'—it must work 'against the pressure' of expected speech. Option C describes meaning arising with 'no expectation-setting language at all,' directly contradicting the parasitism thesis, so it weakens most. Option A is too weak: a single pause within a poem may still rely on surrounding language, leaving the thesis intact. Option B supports the author by tying power to built expectation. Option D actively confirms the author's own example ('remove the pressure and the withholding collapses'), strengthening rather than weakening the claim.

title: The Wound and the Salve
text:
It is an old and seductive claim that tragic drama performs a kind of cleansing. We arrive at the theater burdened by inchoate fears, the argument runs, and we depart unburdened, having watched those fears given shape and then resolved. The spectacle of a great figure's downfall, far from depressing us, is said to discharge our pity and terror as a fever breaks. On this account, the theater is a clinic, and the playwright a physician who heals by inducing, in controlled measure, the very sickness he intends to cure.

I find this picture too tidy by half. Notice what it asks us to believe: that suffering, when artfully arranged, becomes its own antidote. But the consolation theory quietly substitutes the experience of form for the experience of grief. What soothes us, if anything does, is not the misfortune depicted but the symmetry of its depiction—the rhyme, the reversal, the inevitability that hindsight lends to ruin. We are consoled, in other words, by the fiction that catastrophe has a structure. And this is precisely the consolation that actual catastrophe withholds.

Yet I would not go so far as those skeptics who insist that tragedy merely disturbs, that it leaves the spectator more bruised than before and offers nothing but a rehearsal of despair. This view is as one-sided as the one it opposes. For there is a genuine gift in tragic art, though it is not the gift of comfort. It is the gift of company. To watch a figure suffer with eloquence is to learn that suffering need not be mute, that even the worst can be spoken, witnessed, formed into utterance. The tragic hero does not console us by surviving; he consoles us, if at all, by articulating the unsurvivable.

Here the two camps make the same error from opposite directions. The consolers assume that to be moved is to be relieved; the skeptics assume that to be disturbed is to be harmed. Both treat the spectator's distress as a problem to be solved—either dissolved by catharsis or merely inflicted as pain. But the distinctive achievement of tragedy is to make distress intelligible without making it disappear. We leave the theater neither cured nor wounded but, in a sense difficult to name, accompanied.

Consider how poorly the medical metaphor fits. A patient cured of fever is returned to her prior health; she carries nothing of the illness away. But no one leaves a great tragedy as she entered it. Something has been added, not subtracted. The play does not drain us of pity; it deepens our capacity for it. If this is medicine, it is the strangest sort—a treatment that enlarges the very faculty it engages, leaving us more susceptible to sorrow, not less.

This is why I resist calling tragedy consoling, even as I deny that it merely disturbs. Consolation, properly understood, promises that the wound will close. Tragedy promises something harder and, I think, more honest: that the wound, left open, can nonetheless be borne in the presence of others who have looked at it without flinching. The theater does not seal the breach in the world. It teaches us to keep looking at the breach. Whether we call this a salve or a fresh injury depends, perhaps, on what we believed art was for before we sat down. Those who came seeking relief will feel cheated; those who came seeking truth may find that being accompanied in sorrow is its own austere reward.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the traditional "consolation theory" of tragedy holds that the spectator departs the theater:
options: more deeply susceptible to sorrow than before arriving
unburdened, having had pity and terror discharged
accompanied but neither cured nor wounded
convinced that catastrophe lacks any genuine structure
questions.explanation: The first paragraph states the consolation view: we arrive "burdened by inchoate fears" and "depart unburdened, having watched those fears given shape and then resolved," with pity and terror discharged "as a fever breaks." Option B paraphrases this directly. Option A reverses it—deepened susceptibility is the author's own counterclaim (paragraph five), not the consolation theory. Option C is the author's third position ("accompanied"), not the consolation theory he critiques. Option D inverts the author's point that consolation comes from "the fiction that catastrophe has a structure"; the consolation theory relies on structure, it does not deny it.

questions.stem: The passage indicates that, in the author's view, whatever soothing tragedy provides comes primarily from:
options: the moral lesson contained in the hero's downfall
the spectator's relief at having survived the hero
the formal symmetry and inevitability of the depiction
the restoration of the spectator to a prior emotional health
questions.explanation: In paragraph two the author argues that "what soothes us, if anything does, is not the misfortune depicted but the symmetry of its depiction—the rhyme, the reversal, the inevitability," i.e., "the fiction that catastrophe has a structure." Option C captures this. Option A imports a moral lesson the passage never invokes. Option B distorts paragraph three: the author explicitly says the hero "does not console us by surviving." Option D is the medical/cure model the author rejects in paragraph five, where he insists no one leaves "as she entered it" and that something is "added, not subtracted."

questions.stem: The author claims that "the two camps make the same error from opposite directions." This error is best characterized as:
options: assuming the spectator's distress is something to be either resolved or simply inflicted
believing that tragic art conveys no truth about the human condition
denying that audiences feel any genuine emotion in the theater
overestimating the role of dramatic structure in producing emotion
questions.explanation: Paragraph four spells out the shared error: "Both treat the spectator's distress as a problem to be solved—either dissolved by catharsis or merely inflicted as pain." Option A restates this. Option B is wrong because the author credits tragedy with offering truth ("those who came seeking truth"). Option C contradicts the passage, which assumes audiences are genuinely "moved" and "disturbed." Option D misattributes the error; the author himself stresses structure's role, and the camps' shared mistake concerns distress, not an overestimation of structure.

questions.stem: Which statement, if true, would most directly support the author's claim that the medical metaphor "fits poorly"?
options: Audiences report identical relief whether they watch tragedy or comedy
Spectators of great tragedies report a lasting increase in their compassion for others
Tragic plays were historically performed in spaces associated with healing rituals
Viewers forget the plot of a tragedy soon after leaving the theater
questions.explanation: The author's anti-medical argument (paragraph five) is that a cured patient "carries nothing of the illness away," whereas tragedy leaves us "more susceptible to sorrow, not less"—something is "added, not subtracted." Evidence of a lasting increase in compassion (B) confirms that the faculty is enlarged rather than restored to baseline, supporting his claim. Option A concerns comedy, irrelevant to the cure analogy. Option C would arguably strengthen the medical metaphor, not undermine it. Option D suggests nothing is retained, which actually fits the cure model the author rejects, thereby weakening his position.

questions.stem: A reader objects: "The author cannot deny that tragedy consoles while also granting it a 'genuine gift.'" The author would most likely respond that:
options: the gift he affirms is comfort, which is simply another word for consolation
the objection ignores that he ultimately sides with the skeptics
consolation promises a closing wound, whereas tragedy's gift is companionship before an open one
tragedy offers no gift at all, only the appearance of one
questions.explanation: In the final paragraphs the author distinguishes the two: consolation "promises that the wound will close," while tragedy offers "company"—the wound "left open" yet "borne in the presence of others." The gift is real but is not consolation, so the contradiction dissolves (C). Option A is wrong because the author explicitly says the gift "is not the gift of comfort." Option B misreads him; he rejects the skeptics' view that tragedy "merely disturbs." Option D contradicts paragraph three, which affirms a "genuine gift," the gift "of company."

questions.stem: A grief counselor argues that helping a bereaved client means sitting with them in their pain rather than promising the pain will end. This approach is most analogous to the passage's account of:
options: the consolation theory, which discharges pity and terror
the skeptics' view that distress is merely inflicted as harm
tragedy as the author reconceives it—accompaniment without cure
the medical metaphor, in which the sufferer is returned to prior health
questions.explanation: The counselor refuses to promise the pain will end and instead "sits with" the client—mirroring the author's reconceived tragedy, which does not "seal the breach" but lets the wound be "borne in the presence of others" (accompaniment without cure). Option C fits. Option A is the cure model the author rejects—discharging emotion is precisely what the counselor declines to promise. Option B is wrong because the counselor's aim is supportive, not the mere infliction of harm the skeptics describe. Option D is the prior-health restoration the author argues tragedy never delivers.

title: The Backward Glance
text:
Nostalgia has long suffered from poor reputation among those who fancy themselves clear-eyed. To the rationalist, it is a sentimental haze that dignifies a past that never existed; to the progressive, it is reactionary longing, the velvet glove over the fist of resistance to change. Both charges contain truth, and yet both, I will argue, mistake an accidental feature of nostalgia for its essence. The vice they condemn is not nostalgia itself but a particular, lazy species of it.

Consider what the nostalgic person actually does. She does not, in the main, believe the past was perfect; she knows perfectly well that the summers of childhood contained their humiliations and the old neighborhood its cruelties. What she misses is not a set of facts but a quality of attention—the unhurried, unguarded way the world once disclosed itself before she learned to manage it. To call this a falsification of history is to misunderstand the grammar of the emotion. The nostalgic claim is not "things were better then" but "I was differently present then," and the second claim may be entirely accurate.

Here the critic will object that even an accurate emotion can be morally corrosive if it disables action. A people enchanted by an imagined golden age, the argument runs, will squander the present in mourning and refuse the labor of building anything new. This is a serious worry, and I do not dismiss it. But notice that the danger lies not in remembering well but in remembering selectively in the service of grievance. The demagogue's nostalgia is parasitic: it borrows the warmth of genuine memory to license the exclusion of those who supposedly spoiled the idyll. The fault is in the politics, not in the backward glance.

Indeed, I want to press the opposite case. A culture that has lost the capacity for nostalgia has lost something morally indispensable: the ability to feel that the present is not the only possible arrangement of human life. To miss a vanished way of being is to hold open, however faintly, the conviction that things could be otherwise—that the way we now live is contingent rather than fated. The relentlessly forward-facing temperament, by contrast, tends to treat the latest configuration of society as self-evidently superior, and thereby surrenders the very leverage from which the present might be criticized. Properly understood, nostalgia is not the enemy of reform but one of its hidden engines.

This is not to sentimentalize the sentiment. There is a real and corrupting form of nostalgia, and we should name it precisely. It is the nostalgia that refuses to be examined, that converts longing into entitlement and treats the loss of one's own former advantages as a wound inflicted by others. Such nostalgia is not memory but a kind of resentment dressed in memory's clothing. The honest nostalgic, by contrast, holds her longing and her judgment in the same hand: she can mourn what is gone while conceding that it had to go, can love the texture of a vanished world without wishing to restore its injustices.

The moral question, then, is not whether to feel nostalgia but how to feel it. The emotion is neither virtue nor vice in itself; it is raw material that the will shapes toward generosity or grievance. To forbid it altogether, as the austere progressive would, is to impoverish our relation to time. To indulge it uncritically, as the reactionary does, is to weaponize the dead against the living. Between these failures lies the difficult work of remembering truthfully—which is to say, remembering with the past's losses and its costs both held in view.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the central claim made by the honest nostalgic person is best characterized as which of the following?
options: A factual assertion that earlier historical periods were objectively better than the present
A claim about a changed mode of one's own presence to the world rather than about external facts
A demand that vanished social arrangements, including their injustices, be restored
A confession that one's memories of the past are unreliable and largely invented
