When a handful of firms control distribution, the apparent diversity of outputs may rest on a narrowing of inputs—a thousand local remixes of an increasingly small canon.

So the honest answer is neither homogenization nor diversification but a more uncomfortable both. Globalization simultaneously multiplies the surfaces of culture and concentrates its underlying infrastructure. We are offered more variations than ever before, drawn from a source pool that quietly contracts. The error of the homogenization thesis is to look only at the shrinking pool; the error of the diversification thesis is to look only at the proliferating variations. Each captures a real motion and mistakes it for the whole.

What follows is not despair but a sharper question. If cultural vitality depends not merely on the visible variety of expressions but on the plurality of those who control the means of expression, then the metric that matters is not how many flavors are on offer but how many independent hands are doing the cooking. A world of infinite remixes governed by three platforms is not, by that measure, a diverse world; it is a uniform one wearing many costumes. The defenders of local culture, then, should worry less about preserving particular forms—forms have always migrated and mutated—and more about preserving the conditions under which communities retain authorship over their own meanings.

questions.stem: The author's central claim about globalization and culture is best summarized as which of the following?
options: Globalization inevitably homogenizes culture because integrated markets erase local difference.
Globalization both multiplies cultural variety and concentrates the infrastructure that produces it.
Globalization diversifies culture because every borrowed form is locally reinterpreted.
Globalization leaves culture essentially unchanged, since forms have always migrated.
questions.explanation: The author explicitly states the "honest answer is neither homogenization nor diversification but a more uncomfortable both," pairing multiplied "surfaces" with concentrated "infrastructure"—option B. A reflects only the homogenization thesis the author calls an "error" for seeing just the shrinking pool. C reflects only the diversification thesis, also labeled an "error" for seeing only proliferating variations. D contradicts the passage: the author treats culture as changing constantly (forms "migrate and mutate") and never claims it is unchanged; the migration of forms is offered as a reason not to fixate on preserving particular forms, not as evidence of stasis.

questions.stem: According to the passage, when a global cultural product arrives in a particular community, it is typically:
options: consumed exactly as its makers intended
rejected as foreign and quickly discarded
reworked to carry meanings local to that community
stripped of meaning and valued only as a commodity
questions.explanation: The author states reception "is not passive reproduction but active reworking" and that a community pours "its own" meaning into the cultural "vessel"—option C. A directly contradicts the text: products are "rarely consumed as [their] makers intended." B is unsupported; the passage describes absorption and rescripting, not rejection or discarding. D distorts the passage—the commodity/profit concern appears in the later qualification about who owns channels, not as a claim that arriving products are stripped of meaning; the central point of that paragraph is precisely that local meaning is added, not removed.

questions.stem: The author's discussion of "a thousand local remixes of an increasingly small canon" functions primarily to:
options: concede that the hybridity thesis is entirely mistaken
show that apparent diversity can coexist with a narrowing of underlying inputs
prove that homogenization is the only accurate description of globalization
celebrate the creativity unleashed by global distribution networks
questions.explanation: This phrase appears in the qualification of the optimistic view: even genuine local reworking occurs within constraints, so "apparent diversity of outputs may rest on a narrowing of inputs"—option B. A overstates: the author qualifies, not rejects, hybridity, still granting that meanings "are reworked locally." C is too extreme and reverses the author, who insists the answer is "both," not homogenization alone. D inverts the passage's tone—the remixes phrase is a warning about input concentration, not a celebration; the celebratory register belongs to the diversification argument the author is deliberately complicating here.

questions.stem: Which assumption is most necessary to the author's claim that "a world of infinite remixes governed by three platforms" is effectively "uniform"?
options: The number of distinct cultural expressions is the only valid measure of diversity.
Genuine cultural diversity depends on plural, independent control over the means of expression, not merely on the variety of outputs.
Local communities are incapable of reinterpreting imported forms.
Cultural forms cannot migrate or change over time.
questions.explanation: The metaphor only works if diversity is measured by who controls expression rather than by surface variety; the author states the relevant metric is "how many independent hands are doing the cooking," so plural control is the load-bearing assumption—option B. A reverses the author, who explicitly rejects counting variations as the measure. C contradicts the passage, which affirms communities do reinterpret forms. D is also contradicted—the author repeatedly grants that forms migrate and mutate; indeed that concession is why he shifts the worry from forms to conditions of authorship.

questions.stem: A study finds that streaming music in a region shows hundreds of distinct local subgenres, but that 90 percent of all tracks are distributed by two corporations. The author would most likely interpret this finding as:
options: evidence that globalization is purely diversifying
an illustration of multiplied surfaces atop concentrated infrastructure
proof that local subgenres are inauthentic imitations
a sign that cultural homogenization has been fully reversed
questions.explanation: The finding maps precisely onto the author's "both" thesis: visible variety (hundreds of subgenres) resting on concentrated control (two distributors)—"multiplied surfaces" over "concentrated infrastructure," option B. A ignores the concentration the author treats as decisive and revives the diversification error. C imports an authenticity judgment the author never makes—he affirms local reworking is genuine, just constrained. D contradicts him: such concentration is the very thing he warns about, so it cannot signal homogenization "reversed"; if anything he'd call this a uniform world "wearing many costumes."

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most WEAKEN the author's qualification of the diversification thesis?
options: Most popular cultural forms in a given region are distributed by a small number of firms.
Communities consistently reinterpret imported forms to express local concerns.
The raw materials available for cultural borrowing are produced by a large, growing, and independently owned set of sources.
Audiences in different cities draw different meanings from the same film.
questions.explanation: The author's qualification hinges on the claim that diverse outputs rest on a "narrowing of inputs" controlled by few hands. Option C directly undercuts this: if input sources are numerous, growing, and independently owned, the concentration premise collapses, weakening the qualification. A strengthens, not weakens, that qualification by confirming concentrated distribution. B and D restate points the author already accepts (local reinterpretation; divergent readings of one film); since they support the diversification side he is qualifying rather than the qualification itself, they do not undermine his concentration-of-inputs concern.
